1117	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1118	[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across an iterated localisation]
1119	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1120	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1121	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1122	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1123	  \(h \in A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) be non-zero. Write
1124	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1125	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h = A_g[h^{-1}]\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1126	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1127	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f = A[f^{-1}]\).
1128	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1129	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1130	\end{lemma}
1131	
1132	\begin{proof}
1133	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1134	  \leanok
1135	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\),
1136	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1137	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1138	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1139	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1140	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1141	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1142	
1143	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1144	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1145	  \((A_g)_h = A_g[h^{-1}]\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1146	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1147	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1148	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(A_{ga} \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1149	
1150	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1151	  \[
1152	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1153	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1154	  \]
1155	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1156	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1157	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1158	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1159	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1160	
1161	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1162	  isomorphism \(A_{ga} \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1163	  \(T_{ga} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1164	  previous step and promoted to an \(A_{ga}\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1165	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{ga}\) is free over
1166	  \(A_{ga}\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1167	\end{proof}
1168	
1169	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1170	[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1171	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1172	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1173	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1174	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1175	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1176	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1177	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1178	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1179	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1180	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1181	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1182	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1183	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1184	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1185	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1186	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1187	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1188	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1189	  core of the induction).}
1190	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1191	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1192	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1193	
1194	\end{lemma}
1195	
1196	\begin{proof}
1197	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1198	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1199	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1200	        \leanok
1201	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1202	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1203	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1204	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1205	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1206	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1207	
1208	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1209	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1210	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1211	
1212	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1213	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1214	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1215	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1216	  \begin{enumerate}
1217	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1218	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1219	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1220	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1221	      \(P_d\)-modules
1222	      \[
1223	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1224	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1225	      \]
1226	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1227	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1228	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1229	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1230	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1231	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1232	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1233	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1234	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1235	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1236	      to \(A_g\).
1237	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1238	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1239	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1240	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1241	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1242	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1243	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1244	      over \(A_f\).
1245	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1246	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1247	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1248	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1249	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1250	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1251	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1252	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1253	      completes the induction.
1254	  \end{enumerate}
1255	\end{proof}
1256	
1257	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
1258	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
1259	
1260	The following two lemmas package the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms
1261	(flatness and module-finiteness) used by the dévissage chain.
1262	
1263	\begin{lemma}
1264	
1265	
1266	
1267	\leanok
1268	[Flat localization away, finite module]
1269	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
1270	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
1271	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1272	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
1273	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is flat over \(A_f\).
1274	\end{lemma}
1275	\begin{proof}
1276	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1277	  \leanok

1279	\end{proof}

1281	\begin{lemma}
1282	
1283	
1284	
1285	\leanok
1286	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
1287	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}

1289	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}

1291	  finite \(B\)-module with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower
1292	  \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\)
1293	  is free over \(A_f\).

1295	\begin{proof}
1296	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}

1298	  Since \(B\) is module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\),
1299	  transitivity of module-finiteness makes \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module.
1300	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} then gives the required \(f\).
1301	\end{proof}

1303	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1304	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1305	
1306	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1307	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1308	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1309	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1310	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1311	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1312	restricted sheaf over the base open.
1313	
1314	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness is local on a finite spanning family]
1315	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1316	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_localizationSpan_finite}
1317	  \mathlibok
1318	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Submodule}).}
1319	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(N\) an \(R\)-module, and \(t \subseteq R\) a
1320	  finite family with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = \top\) (the \(t\) generate the unit
1321	  ideal). Suppose that for each \(g \in t\) the localization \(N_g\) is a finite
1322	  module over the localization \(R_g\). Then \(N\) is a finite \(R\)-module.
1323	\end{lemma}
1324	
1325	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1326	[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
1327	  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
1328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
1329	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
1330	  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
1331	  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
1332	  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
1333	  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
1334	  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
1335	  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
1336	  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1337	\end{lemma}
1338	
1339	\begin{proof}
1340	
1341	\leanok
1342	  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
1343	  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
1344	  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
1345	  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
1346	  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
1347	  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
1348	  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
1349	  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
1350	  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
1351	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
1352	  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1353	\end{proof}
1354	
1355	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1356	[Half 1 — G1 locality reduction over a finite basic-open cover]
1357	  \label{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1358	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1359	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1360	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1361	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof, gluing step
1362	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2107-L2109).
1363	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1364	  % "Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$. Hence we conclude by
1365	  %  Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover}."
1366	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (gluing step,
1367	  Algebra Lemma \texttt{lemma-cover}).}
1368	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(X\), and
1369	  \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) is a finite
1370	  family with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) (so the basic opens \(D(g)\),
1371	  \(g \in t\), cover \(W\)), and that for each \(g \in t\) the section module
1372	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). Then \(\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1373	  is a finite \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1374	\end{lemma}
1375	
1376	\begin{proof}
1377	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1378	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1379	        \leanok
1380	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (gluing step).}
1381	  By the local-on-a-spanning-family criterion \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1382	  it suffices to show, for each \(g \in t\), that the localization of
1383	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\) is finite over the localization
1384	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). Fix \(g \in t\). The basic open \(D(g)\) is affine, and the
1385	  restriction \(\Gamma(X, W) \to \Gamma(X, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\) as
1386	  the away-localization \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1387	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); moreover the section restriction
1388	  \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the
1389	  localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1390	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). Thus \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is a
1391	  model of the localized module, finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = (\Gamma(X, W))_g\)

1393	  \cref{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer} carries this finiteness to the
1394	  canonical localized module of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at the powers of \(g\), over
1395	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). This supplies the per-element hypothesis, and
1396	  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan} concludes that \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is
1397	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).

1400	\subsection{Transport engine for generating sections}
1401	
1402	The restriction-of-generators lemma below is assembled from a generic transport
1403	principle: a finite generating family of a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}\) pushes
1404	forward to a finite generating family of \(F(\mathcal{M})\) along any functor \(F\) of
1405	sheaves of modules that preserves colimits and carries the structure sheaf to the
1406	structure sheaf. The three blocks of this subsection package that principle (the
1407	generating family, its index type, and the finiteness bookkeeping). They are
1408	project-bespoke: Mathlib provides the analogous transport only for full presentations
1409	(with kernel data), not for bare generating families.
1410	
1411	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1412	[Transport of a generating family along a colimit-preserving functor]
1413	  \label{lem:genSections_map}
1414	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map}
1415	  Let \(F : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to \mathrm{Mod}\,S\) be a functor of sheaves of modules
1416	  that preserves colimits and is equipped with an isomorphism
1417	  \(\eta : F(\OO_R) \xrightarrow{\sim} \OO_S\) carrying the unit (structure sheaf) of the
1418	  source to that of the target. Given a generating family \(\sigma\) of \(\mathcal{M}\)
1419	  with index set \(I = \sigma.I\) and epimorphism
1420	  \(\sigma.\pi : \OO_R^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{M}\), the images
1421	  \((\mathrm{mapFree}\,F\,\eta\,I)^{-1} \circ F(\sigma.\pi)\) form a generating family of
1422	  \(F(\mathcal{M})\) with the same index set \(I\). The map is an epimorphism because it
1423	  is the composite of an isomorphism with \(F(\sigma.\pi)\), and \(F\) preserves the
1424	  epimorphism \(\sigma.\pi\).
1425	\end{lemma}
1426	
1427	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1428	[The transported family keeps its index type]
1429	  \label{lem:genSections_map_I}
1430	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map_I}
1431	  \uses{lem:genSections_map}
1432	  The index set of the transported generating family of \cref{lem:genSections_map} equals
1433	  the index set of the original: \((\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\,F\,\eta)\!.I = \sigma.I\). This
1434	  holds definitionally.
1435	\end{lemma}
1436	
1437	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1438	[Finiteness survives transport]
1439	  \label{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1440	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map_isFiniteType}
1441	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I}
1442	  If the original generating family \(\sigma\) is of finite type (its index set is finite),
1443	  then so is the transported family \(\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\,F\,\eta\): the index type is
1444	  unchanged by \cref{lem:genSections_map_I}, hence still finite.
1445	\end{lemma}
1446	
1447	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1448	[Half 2, seam 1a — local generators restrict to a smaller open]
1449	  \label{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1450	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1451	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1452	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
1453	  Suppose that on an open \(Y \subseteq X\) the restriction \(\F|_Y\) is
1454	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Then for any open \(V \le Y\) the
1455	  restricted family — the images of those generators under the section-restriction
1456	  \(\Gamma(\F, Y) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) — is again a finite generating family of
1457	  \(\F|_V\). Generation survives passage to the smaller open (a family that generates
1458	  every stalk over \(Y\) generates every stalk over \(V\)), and the index set, and hence
1459	  finiteness, is unchanged.
1460	\end{lemma}
1461	
1462	\begin{proof}
1463	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1464	  \leanok
1465	  A family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_Y\) precisely when the
1466	  induced map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) is surjective on every stalk
1467	  \(\F_y\,(y \in Y)\). Restricting along the inclusion \(V \le Y\) the
1468	  generators map to \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\), and for a point \(x \in V\) the stalk
1469	  \(\F_x\) and the comparison map agree with the value at \(x\) computed over \(Y\), since
1470	  stalks are insensitive to shrinking the ambient open. Hence the induced map
1471	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \to \F|_V\) is surjective on every stalk over \(V\), so
1472	  \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_V\). The index set \(I\) is the same
1473	  finite set, so finiteness is preserved. Concretely the restricted family is obtained by
1474	  transporting the generating family along the (colimit-preserving, unit-preserving)
1475	  restriction functors via the transport engine \cref{lem:genSections_map}, whose index
1476	  type is unchanged (\cref{lem:genSections_map_I}) and which preserves finiteness
1477	  (\cref{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}).
1478	\end{proof}
1479	
1480	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1481	[Half 2, seam 1b — finite standard-basic-open subcover of an affine open]
1482	  \label{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1483	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1484	  Let \(W\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), with the canonical identification
1485	  \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\). Given an arbitrary open cover \(\{U_\alpha\}\) of \(W\),
1486	  there is a finite family \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1487	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that each basic open \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\)) is
1488	  contained in some cover member \(U_\alpha\). In particular the \(D(g)\,(g \in t)\) form a
1489	  finite standard-basic-open subcover of \(W\) refining \(\{U_\alpha\}\).
1490	\end{lemma}
1491	
1492	\begin{proof}
1493	
1494	\leanok
1495	  Under \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) the basic opens \(D(g)\,(g \in \Gamma(X, W))\) form a
1496	  basis of the topology (\(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}\)). Hence each
1497	  cover member \(U_\alpha\) is a union of basic opens, and the collection of all basic
1498	  opens \(D(g)\) that are contained in some \(U_\alpha\) still covers \(W\). The affine
1499	  scheme \(W\) is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens
1500	  \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already cover \(W\); set
1501	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\), a finite family, each \(D(g_i)\)
1502	  contained in some \(U_\alpha\) by construction. That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
1503	  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) — i.e. no prime ideal contains all the \(g_i\) — is exactly
1504	  the condition \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\).
1505	\end{proof}
1506	
1507	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]
1508	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1510	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a sheaf of modules on \(Y\). Then
1511	  \(\F\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1512	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1513	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\). No quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is assumed:
1514	  the statement holds for arbitrary sheaves of modules on \(Y\), and applies in particular
1515	  to the restrictions \(\F|_{D(g)}\) arising in the cover assembly.
1516	\end{lemma}
1517	
1518	\begin{proof}
1519	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1520	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1521	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1522	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1523	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1524	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1525	  finite generating family.
1526	\end{proof}
1527	
1528	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1529	[Half 2, seam 1 — finite type yields a finite cover of globally-generated affines]
1530	  \label{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1531	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1532	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1533	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2101-L2107).
1534	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1535	  % "Assume $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. This means
1536	  %  there exists an open covering of $X$ such that $\widetilde M$ restricted to
1537	  %  the members of this covering is globally generated by finitely many sections.
1538	  %  Thus there also exists a standard open covering
1539	  %  $X = \bigcup_{i = 1, \ldots, n} D(f_i)$ such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$
1540	  %  is generated by finitely many sections."
1541	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1542	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1543	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (standard
1544	  cover of generated affines).}
1545	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a sheaf of modules of finite type,
1546	  and \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then there is a finite family
1547	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that for
1548	  each \(g \in t\) the basic open \(D(g)\) is affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) is globally
1549	  generated by finitely many sections; equivalently, for each \(g \in t\) there is
1550	  a finite index set \(I_g\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g)}\)-modules
1551	  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\).
1552	\end{lemma}
1553	
1554	\begin{proof}
1555	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1556	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1557	        \leanok
1558	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (standard cover of generated affines).}
1559	  Finite type of \(\F\) means that \(X\) has an open covering \(\{Y_\alpha\}\) on each
1560	  member of which \(\F\) carries a finite generating family of sections. The intersections
1561	  \(\{Y_\alpha \cap W\}\) form an open cover of the affine \(W\). By the finite standard
1562	  subcover refinement \cref{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover} there is a finite family
1563	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1564	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) and each basic open \(D(g_i)\) contained in some
1565	  \(Y_\alpha \cap W\) — in particular \(D(g_i) \subseteq W\) is affine, and \(D(g_i)\)
1566	  embeds by an open immersion into the member \(Y_\alpha\) on which \(\F\) is finitely
1567	  generated. By the restriction-of-generators lemma
1568	  \cref{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict} the finite generating family on \(Y_\alpha\)
1569	  restricts to a finite generating family of \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\), so \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1570	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Finally, by the free-epimorphism
1571	  reformulation \cref{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}, this is equivalent to the existence
1572	  of a finite index set \(I_{g_i}\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g_i)}\)-modules
1573	  \(\OO_{D(g_i)}^{\oplus I_{g_i}} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g_i)}\). That the \(D(g_i)\)
1574	  cover \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) is exactly \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\). This is
1575	  Stacks' passage from the local-generation datum to a standard finite cover of generated
1576	  affines.
1577	\end{proof}

1579	\begin{lemma}[Affine $\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma$ adjunction with its $\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ counit]
1580	  \label{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib}
1581	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.adjunction}
1582	  \mathlibok
1583	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}).}
1584	  On the affine scheme \(\Spec R\) the associated-sheaf functor
1585	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules}\) is left adjoint to the
1586	  affine section functor \(\Gamma : (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules} \to \mathrm{Mod}\,R\) (Mathlib's
1587	  \(\mathrm{moduleSpecΓFunctor}\), evaluation of a sheaf of modules at the top open). The counit of
1588	  this adjunction is the natural transformation
1589	  \(\Gamma(-)\,\widetilde{\phantom{x}} \to \mathrm{id}\) whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\)
1590	  is \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \top)} \to \mathcal{M}\), and
1591	  the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful.
1592	\end{lemma}

1594	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1595	[Half 2, seam 2 — $\Gamma$ on affine quasi-coherent modules sends epis to surjections]
1596	  \label{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1597	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1598	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1599	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1600	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1601	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2106-L2107) — the implication
1602	  % "$\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ generated by finitely many sections $\Rightarrow$
1603	  % $M_{f_i}$ finitely generated" is exactly section-level surjectivity of a sheaf
1604	  % epi over an affine.
1605	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1606	  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.
1607	  %  Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$."
1608	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (sections of a
1609	  generated sheaf over an affine).}
1610	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme, with \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) and
1611	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1612	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Then the induced map on sections
1613	  \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) is a surjective
1614	  \(B\)-module map.
1615	\end{lemma}
1616	
1617	\begin{proof}
1618	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1619	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1620	    \leanok
1621	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1622	  affine).}
1623	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1624	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1625	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1626	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1627	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1628	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1629	
1630	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1631	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1632	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1633	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1634	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1635	  \[
1636	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1637	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1638	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1639	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1640	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1641	  \]
1642	  The right-hand side is a composite of an isomorphism, the epimorphism \(\pi\), and an
1643	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1644	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1645	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1646	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
1647	
1648	  This is the structural content of the vanishing of higher quasi-coherent cohomology on an
1649	  affine — the failure of \(\Gamma\) to be right exact would obstruct exactly this surjectivity —
1650	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.
1651	\end{proof}
1652	
1653	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1654	[Half 2, seam 2$+$3 — a qcoh epimorphism with finite source sections has finite target sections]
1655	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}
1656	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}
1657	  \uses{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi, lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}
1658	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB
1659	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2094-L2107).
1660	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1661	  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme. The quasi-coherent sheaf of
1662	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1663	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
1664	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1665	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), write \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) with
1666	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1667	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Suppose the source has finite sections:
1668	  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V)\) is a finite \(B\)-module. Then \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite
1669	  \(B\)-module.
1670	\end{lemma}
1671	
1672	\begin{proof}
1673	  \uses{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi, lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}
1674	  \leanok
1675	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB.}
1676	  Apply seam 2 (\cref{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}) to the epimorphism
1677	  \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) over the affine \(V\): the section functor yields a
1678	  surjective \(B\)-module map \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\).
1679	  The source \(\Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V)\) is a finite \(B\)-module by hypothesis. A finite
1680	  module surjecting onto \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) forces \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) to be finite, by
1681	  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}. Hence \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite
1682	  \(B\)-module.
1683	\end{proof}

1685	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1686	[Half 2 base case — a finite-free qcoh epimorphism has finite affine sections]
1687	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi}
1688	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_free_epi}
1689	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib}
1690	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1691	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2106-L2107) — "$\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$
1692	  % generated by finitely many sections $\Rightarrow$ $M_{f_i}$ finitely generated".
1693	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1694	  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.
1695	  %  Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$."
1696	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (free generation
1697	  over an affine).}
1698	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), write \(R = \Gamma(X, V)\) with
1699	  \(V \cong \Spec R\), and let \(\F\) be a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules. Suppose there
1700	  is a finite \(R\)-module \(N\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_V\)-modules
1701	  \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F\) out of the associated sheaf of \(N\) (the case
1702	  \(N = R^{\oplus I}\), \(I\) finite, being the free-generation datum
1703	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\)). Then \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite
1704	  \(R\)-module.
1705	\end{lemma}
1706	
1707	\begin{proof}
1708	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib}
1709	  \leanok
1710	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB.}
1711	  This is the free-source specialisation of
1712	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}; what remains is to discharge that
1713	  lemma's two source-side hypotheses for \(\mathcal{G} = \widetilde{N}\). By the affine
1714	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma\) adjunction \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the unit
1715	  at \(N\) is an isomorphism \(N \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gamma(\widetilde{N}, V)\) (the left
1716	  adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is fully faithful), so \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, V)\) is a
1717	  finite \(R\)-module because \(N\) is; and the counit component at \(\widetilde{N}\),
1718	  namely \(\widetilde{N}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\), is an isomorphism (a fully-faithful left
1719	  adjoint has invertible counit on its image), supplying the source quasi-coherence
1720	  needed by seam 2. With both source hypotheses in hand,
1721	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated} applied to
1722	  \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F\) yields that \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite
1723	  \(R\)-module.
1724	\end{proof}

1726	\begin{lemma}
1727	
1728	\leanok
1729	[Finiteness transports across a ring isomorphism and a semilinear additive map]
1730	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear}
1731	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear}
1732	  Let \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\) be a ring isomorphism and
1733	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) an additive isomorphism that is \(\sigma\)-semilinear,
1734	  i.e. \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\) for all \(r \in R\), \(m \in M\). If
1735	  \(M\) is a finite \(R\)-module then \(M'\) is a finite \(R'\)-module.
1736	\end{lemma}
1737	
1738	\begin{proof}
1739	
1740	\leanok
1741	  Pick a finite \(R\)-spanning set \(T \subseteq M\). The image \(e(T)\) is finite, and
1742	  it \(R'\)-spans \(M'\): for \(m' \in M'\) write \(m' = e(m)\) with
1743	  \(m = \sum_i r_i t_i\) (\(t_i \in T\)); then
1744	  \(m' = e(\sum_i r_i t_i) = \sum_i \sigma(r_i)\, e(t_i)\) by semilinearity, an
1745	  \(R'\)-combination of \(e(T)\). Hence the top submodule of \(M'\) is finitely
1746	  generated, i.e. \(M'\) is a finite \(R'\)-module.
1747	\end{proof}
1748	
1749	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1750	[G1 base case — finite affine sections from a finite generating family]
1751	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1752	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1753	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi, lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
1754	        lem:module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear}
1755	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules, and
1756	  \(D \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose the restriction
1757	  \(\F|_D = (\mathrm{pullback}\,D.\iota)^{*}\F\) carries a finite generating family
1758	  \(\sigma\), with index set \(I = \sigma.I\). Then the section module
1759	  \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, D)\). This is the affine base
1760	  case of \(G1\): once the generators are in hand on an affine open, finiteness of
1761	  sections is purely an affine (Spec-side) statement. (Only quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is
1762	  needed — the finite-generation datum is carried by \(\sigma\) directly, so no separate
1763	  finite-type hypothesis on \(\F\) is required.)
1764	\end{lemma}
1765	
1766	\begin{proof}
1767	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi, lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
1768	        lem:module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1769	        \leanok
1770	  The content is the transport of the finite generating datum
1771	  from the category of sheaves of modules over the affine open \(D\) to the affine
1772	  scheme \(\Spec \Gamma(X, D)\), where the affine base case applies. Write
1773	  \(R = \Gamma(X, D)\) and use the canonical identification
1774	  \(D \cong \Spec R\) (\(\mathrm{isoSpec}\)). The argument has three steps.
1775	
1776	  \emph{(a) Quasi-coherence on \(\Spec R\).} Transport \(\F|_D\) along
1777	  \(\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1}\) to a sheaf of modules \(\F'\) on \(\Spec R\). Finite type of
1778	  \(\F\) is inherited by the restriction, and quasi-coherence of \(\F'\) is the assertion
1779	  that its counit \(\F'.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism; this is the affine
1780	  quasi-coherence criterion \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ} fed by the basic-open
1781	  localization datum \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1782	
1783	  \emph{(b) A finite free epimorphism over \(\Spec R\).} The generating family \(\sigma\)
1784	  furnishes an epimorphism \(\OO_D^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_D\). Transporting along
1785	  \(\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1}\) and using that the pullback of the free module along an
1786	  isomorphism is again free (\((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1})^{*}\OO_D^{\oplus I}
1787	  \cong \OO_{\Spec R}^{\oplus I}\)), together with the identification of the free sheaf with
1788	  the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\) of \(N = R^{\oplus I}\) (the associated-sheaf
1789	  functor preserves coproducts and \(\widetilde{R} \cong \OO_{\Spec R}\)), gives an
1790	  epimorphism \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F'\) with \(N = R^{\oplus I}\) a finite
1791	  \(R\)-module (\(I\) finite).
1792	
1793	  \emph{(c) Identification of section modules.} The affine global-sections functor sends
1794	  \(\F'\) to \(\Gamma(\F', \top)\), which the transport identifies with \(\Gamma(\F, D)\)
1795	  as \(R = \Gamma(X, D)\)-modules.
1796	
1797	  Now the affine base case \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi}, applied to the finite-free
1798	  quasi-coherent epimorphism \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F'\) over \(\Spec R\),
1799	  shows \(\Gamma(\F', \top)\) is a finite \(R\)-module; by (c) this is \(\Gamma(\F, D)\)
1800	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, D)\).
1801	\end{proof}

1803	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1804	[G1 — finite-type quasi-coherent sheaf has finite affine sections]
1805	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1806	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1807	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover,
1808	        lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated,
1809	        lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi,
1810	        lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1811	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB
1812	  % (Lemma "Characterizing modules of finite type", \S "Characterizing modules of
1813	  % finite type and finite presentation")
1814	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2092-L2098).
1815	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1816	  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
1817	  %  The quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1818	  %  $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1819	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
1820	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1821	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1822	  modules with \(\F\) of finite type. Then for every affine open \(W \subseteq X\), the
1823	  section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1824	\end{lemma}
1825	
1826	\begin{proof}
1827	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover,
1828	        lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated,
1829	        lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi,
1830	        lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1831	        \leanok
1832	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
1833	  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
1834	  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
1835	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1836	  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\). Cover extraction
1837	  \cref{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated} gives a finite
1838	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) spanning the unit ideal, with each \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\))
1839	  affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) globally generated by finitely many sections, equivalently a
1840	  finite-free epimorphism \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\). The
1841	  free-epi base case \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi} applied on each affine \(D(g)\)
1842	  makes \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). The locality reduction
1843	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover} then glues these per-member

976	  data to \(V_{IJK}\)) the Cramer inverses of the localised minors satisfy the
977	  multiplicative cocycle identity

979	    (X^J_K)^{-1}\,(X^I_J)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1}.
980	  \]

986	    lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_cocycle}
987	  Write all matrices over the common triple-overlap ring. The image matrix of the
988	  transition \(\theta_{I,J}\) is \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\), and its \(K\)-minor is
989	  computed by restricting to the \(K\)-indexed columns; since taking a column
990	  submatrix commutes with left multiplication (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}),
991	  \[
992	    X^J_K \;=\; \bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr)_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_K .
993	  \]

995	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}), so this product of invertibles inverts

998	    (X^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1}\,(X^I_J).
999	  \]

1001	  via the two-sided Cramer identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}, the
1002	  cancellation packaged in \cref{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}) gives
1003	  \((X^J_K)^{-1} (X^I_J)^{-1} = (X^I_K)^{-1}\). The agreement of the three image
1004	  matrices over the triple overlap that licenses these manipulations is the content of
1005	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}, the matrix form of the scheme-level cocycle
1006	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}.

1012	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1013	  Let \(p : T \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(a \in \Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\) a
1014	  global function, with comorphism on global sections
1015	  \(p^{\sharp} : \Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S) \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)\).
1016	  Write \(q : p^{*}\mathbf{1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathbf{1}\) for the unit-pullback comparison
1017	  (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index). Then the pullback of the scalar endomorphism
1018	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\) is conjugate, through \(q\), to the scalar endomorphism of the
1019	  base-changed function:
1020	  \[
1021	    p^{*}\bigl(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\bigr)
1022	      \;=\; q^{-1} \,\circ\, \mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(p^{\sharp} a\bigr) \,\circ\, q
1023	  \]
1024	  as endomorphisms of \(p^{*}\mathbf{1}\); equivalently
1025	  \(q \circ p^{*}(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(p^{\sharp} a) \circ q\). This is the

1027	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}.

1032	  by the global section \(a\) on the unit module \(\mathbf 1 = \mathcal{O}_S\). Pulling back
1033	  along \(p\) sends multiplication by \(a\) to multiplication by its image, and the comorphism
1034	  \(p^{\sharp}\) is precisely the action of pullback on global functions, so
1035	  \(p^{*}(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a))\) is multiplication by \(p^{\sharp}a\) on \(p^{*}\mathbf 1\). The
1036	  comparison \(q\) is an isomorphism of unit modules intertwining this multiplication on
1037	  \(p^{*}\mathbf 1\) with multiplication by \(p^{\sharp}a\) on \(\mathbf 1\); conjugating back
1038	  along \(q\) therefore yields the claimed identity. The verification is a single computation on
1039	  the unit module, where multiplication by a function commutes with the natural comparison
1040	  \(q\).

1200	    def:gr_away_incl_right, lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left,
1201	    lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_right, lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_transition}
1202	  Fix indices \(I, J, K\) and the triple overlap \(V_{IJK}\) of the chart cover
1203	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}, \cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}).
1204	  The scheme-pullback base-change map on global sections
1205	  \(\Gamma(U^I_J, \mathcal{O}) \to \Gamma(V_{IJK}, \mathcal{O})\) induced by the first projection
1206	  \(p^{IJ}_{IJK}\), and likewise the maps induced by the second projection and by the triple
1207	  transition \(t'_{IJK}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}), are identified -- through the natural identification of global sections of an affine scheme
1208	  with its coordinate ring and the fact that the projections and triple transition are the
1209	  \(\Spec\) morphisms of the coordinate-ring homomorphisms by which the Grassmannian glue datum
1210	  (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) is assembled -- with the ring homomorphisms
1211	  \(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}),
1212	  \(\mathrm{awayInclRight}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right}) and \(\Theta_{IJ}\)
1213	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) over which the matrix cocycle
1214	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} is stated. Consequently the entrywise image of a Cramer
1215	  inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) under the geometric base-change map agrees with its image under the
1216	  corresponding coordinate-ring homomorphism, so \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} applies
1217	  to the base-changed matrices.

1221	    lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_transition}

1223	  handled by \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left}, identifying its global-sections base-change

1225	  \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_right}, identifying it with \(\mathrm{awayInclRight}\); and the
1226	  triple transition \(t'_{IJK}\) by \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_transition}, identifying it
1227	  with \(\Theta_{IJ}\). Each bridge already records the agreement of the entrywise image of the
1228	  relevant Cramer inverse under the geometric base-change with its image under the named ring
1229	  homomorphism over which \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} is stated, so assembling the three
1230	  gives the claim.

1233	\begin{lemma}[Transport and endpoint alignment of the bundle transitions]
1234	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport}
1235	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_cocycle_transport}
1236	  \uses{def:gr_bundleTransition, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,
1237	    lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback, lem:gr_baseChange_bridge,
1238	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges,
1239	    def:modules_pullbackComp, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1240	  Fix indices \(I, J, K\) with triple overlap \(V_{IJK} = U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K\),
1241	  and let \(\widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K}, \widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I}, \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}\) be
1242	  the base-change transports (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the
1243	  three bundle transitions to \(V_{IJK}\). Under the free-pullback comparisons
1244	  (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}) and the endpoint bridges
1245	  (\cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}), each transported transition is identified with the
1246	  matrix automorphism of the corresponding base-changed Cramer inverse on the common
1247	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{IJK}}^{\,d}\); granting the matrix identity
1248	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix}, the composite satisfies
1249	  \[
1250	    \widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I} \circ \widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K}
1251	      \;=\; \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}
1252	  \]
1253	  as isomorphisms of sheaves of modules on \(V_{IJK}\). This is the substantive
1254	  transport step: the alignment of the three transports' domains and codomains.
1255	\end{lemma}
1256	\begin{proof}
1257	  \uses{def:gr_bundleTransition, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,
1258	    lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback, lem:gr_baseChange_bridge,
1259	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges,
1260	    def:modules_pullbackComp, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1261	  This is pure assembly of the two infrastructure pieces over the endpoint bridges. Each
1262	  bundle transition is, by \cref{def:gr_bundleTransition}, \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}\) of a
1263	  localised Cramer inverse conjugated by the free-pullback comparisons
1264	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}; its base-change transport
1265	  (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) reassociates the iterated pullbacks via
1266	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, and the three endpoint bridges \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
1267	  pin the source, middle and target sheaves to a single \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{IJK}}^{\,d}\). By
1268	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback} each transport then equals \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\) of the
1269	  base-changed Cramer inverse, and by \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge} those base-changed matrices
1270	  are exactly \((X^I_J)^{-1}, (X^J_K)^{-1}, (X^I_K)^{-1}\) over the common triple-overlap ring of
1271	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix}. The linkage \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul} composes the
1272	  first two to \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\bigl((X^J_K)^{-1}(X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr)\), and
1273	  by the matrix cocycle identity \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} the argument equals
1274	  \((X^I_K)^{-1}\), so the composite equals \(\widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}\); the bridges make both
1275	  sides isomorphisms between the same pair of sheaves on \(V_{IJK}\).
1276	\end{proof}

1278	\begin{lemma}[Triple-overlap multiplicativity of the bundle transition (C2)]
1279	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_mul}
1280	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_cocycle}
1281	  \uses{def:gr_bundleTransition, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,
1282	    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport, lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport,
1283	    lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1284	  Over each triple overlap \(V_{IJK} = U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K\) the base-change transports
1285	  (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three bundle transitions satisfy
1286	  \[
1287	    \widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I} \circ \widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K}
1288	      \;=\; \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J},
1289	  \]
1290	  the module-level form of the descent condition (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} for
1291	  the bundle data \((g_{I,J})\). This is the \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued multiplicative cocycle
1292	  of \(\mathcal{U}\).
1293	\end{lemma}
1294	\begin{proof}
1295	  \uses{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,
1296	    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport}
1297	  The statement decomposes into three independent steps. First, the matrix-algebra core
1298	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} establishes the Cramer-inverse cocycle
1299	  \((X^J_K)^{-1}(X^I_J)^{-1} = (X^I_K)^{-1}\) over the triple overlap. Second, the linkage
1300	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul} records that composition of matrix automorphisms realises
1301	  matrix multiplication. Third, the transport step \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport}
1302	  carries the three transitions to the common overlap \(V_{IJK}\), aligns their source,
1303	  middle, and target sheaves through the base-change transport and the endpoint bridges, and
1304	  -- feeding it the matrix identity through the linkage -- yields exactly the stated equality
1305	  \(\widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I} \circ \widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K} = \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}\).
1306	\end{proof}

1308	\begin{definition}
1309	
1310	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
1311	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
1312	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
1313	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
1314	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
1315	    def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, def:gr_bundleTransition,
1316	    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_mul,
1317	    lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1318	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1319	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
1320	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
1321	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1322	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
1323	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
1324	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
1325	  %  by putting
1326	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
1327	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1328	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
1329	  \[
1330	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
1331	  \]
1332	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
1333	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
1334	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
1335	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
1336	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
1337	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
1338	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
1339	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
1340	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
1341	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
1342	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
1343	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
1344	\end{definition}
1345	
1346	The tautological quotient \(u\) is assembled from the chart quotients \(u^I\) by the
1347	section-level descent \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom}; its one remaining content is the
1348	\emph{overlap-compatibility} of the family \((u^I)\) with the bundle gluing
1349	\((g_{I,J})\). The next four blocks isolate that compatibility: a rectangular analogue of
1350	the square matrix-endomorphism API (\cref{def:gr_matrixEnd},
1351	\cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}) for the
1352	\(r \to d\) chart quotient (\cref{def:gr_matrixEndRect},
1353	\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}), the
1354	adjunction transposition that turns the descent condition into a pullback-level identity
1355	(\cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose}), and the matrix-core assembly
1356	that closes it (\cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotient_overlap}).
1357	
1358	\subsection{Matrix-calculus toolbox}
1359	
1360	The chart-cover lemma (\cref{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}) and the overlap compatibility of
1361	\cref{def:grPointOfRankQuotient} both rest on a small calculus of \emph{rectangular} matrix
1362	homomorphisms of free sheaves, generalising the square API
1363	(\cref{def:gr_matrixEnd}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}). The
1364	blocks below collect that toolbox: the bridge to the square case, the identity and
1365	composition laws, injectivity of the matrix presentation, the column-restriction law, the
1366	affine splitting of epimorphisms of free sheaves and the resulting matrix right inverse, the
1367	field-level minor extraction, and the presented-matrix machinery that packages a quotient as
1368	a matrix along a morphism and proves Nitsure's change-of-basis identity. They are
1369	project-bespoke infrastructure.
1370	
1371	\begin{lemma}[Square and rectangular matrix homomorphisms coincide]
1372	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_eq_matrixEndRect}
1373	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_eq_matrixEndRect}
1374	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1375	  For a square matrix \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), the
1376	  square and rectangular matrix homomorphisms agree:
1377	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M) = \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\).
1378	\end{lemma}
1379	\begin{proof}
1380	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1381	  Both are the morphism assembled from the same biproduct components
1382	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{k,\ell})\) over the index set \(\{1,\dots,d\}\); the two
1383	  constructions are literally the same, so the equality holds by definition.
1384	\end{proof}
1385	
1386	\begin{lemma}[Identity law for \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\)]
1387	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one}
1388	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_one}
1389	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_matrixEnd_one, lem:gr_matrixEnd_eq_matrixEndRect}
1390	  The rectangular homomorphism of the identity matrix is the identity morphism:
1391	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}}\).
1392	\end{lemma}
1393	\begin{proof}
1394	  \uses{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one, lem:gr_matrixEnd_eq_matrixEndRect}
1395	  By \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_eq_matrixEndRect} the left side equals \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(1)\),
1396	  which is the identity by \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}.
1397	\end{proof}
1398	
1399	\begin{lemma}[Fully rectangular composition law]
1400	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp_rect}
1401	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_comp_rect}
1402	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_sum,
1403	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_add, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}
1404	  For \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{e\times n}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\) and
1405	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), composing two
1406	  rectangular homomorphisms realises the matrix product, the order reversed by the
1407	  contravariant component indexing exactly as in \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}: the
1408	  composite
1409	  \[
1410	    \mathcal{O}_S^{\,n} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(A)} \mathcal{O}_S^{\,e}
1411	      \xrightarrow{\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(B)} \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}
1412	  \]
1413	  equals \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(B \cdot A)\) (the \(d \times n\) product).
1414	\end{lemma}
1415	\begin{proof}
1416	  \uses{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_sum, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
1417	  The same biproduct-extensionality computation as \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}, with the
1418	  middle biproduct indexed by \(\{1,\dots,e\}\). The \((k,\ell)\)-component of the composite
1419	  is \(\sum_m \mathrm{scalarEnd}(B_{k,m})\circ\mathrm{scalarEnd}(A_{m,\ell})\); each composite
1420	  is the scalar endomorphism of the product (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}) and a finite sum
1421	  of scalar endomorphisms is the scalar endomorphism of the sum (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_sum},
1422	  \cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}), so the component is
1423	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}((B\cdot A)_{k,\ell})\), the \((k,\ell)\)-component of
1424	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(B\cdot A)\).
1425	\end{proof}
1426	
1427	\begin{lemma}[Uniqueness of the presenting matrix]
1428	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}
1429	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_injective}
1430	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, def:gr_scalarEnd}
1431	  The matrix presentation is injective: if
1432	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M) = \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(N)\) for
1433	  \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), then \(M = N\).
1434	\end{lemma}
1435	\begin{proof}
1436	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
1437	  Extract the \((p,j)\)-entry by pre-composing with the free-basis inclusion of the
1438	  \(j\)-th summand and post-composing with the projection onto the \(p\)-th summand: this
1439	  isolates the scalar endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{p,j})\) from
1440	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\). Equal homomorphisms therefore give
1441	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{p,j}) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(N_{p,j})\); and \(a \mapsto
1442	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\) is injective (its inverse reads off the underlying section), whence
1443	  \(M_{p,j} = N_{p,j}\) for all \(p, j\).
1444	\end{proof}
1445	
1446	\begin{lemma}[Column restriction against the index inclusion]
1447	  \label{lem:gr_freeMap_matrixEndRect}
1448	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.freeMap_matrixEndRect}
1449	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1450	  For an index map \(g : \{1,\dots,e\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) and
1451	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), pre-composing the
1452	  rectangular homomorphism with the free index inclusion
1453	  \(\mathrm{freeMap}(g) : \mathcal{O}_S^{\,e} \to \mathcal{O}_S^{\,r}\) restricts to the
1454	  \(g\)-columns:
1455	  \[
1456	    \mathrm{freeMap}(g) \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)
1457	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEndRect}\bigl(M_{\bullet,\, g}\bigr),
1458	  \]
1459	  where \(M_{\bullet,\, g}\) is the \(d \times e\) submatrix with \(\ell\)-th column the
1460	  \(g(\ell)\)-th column of \(M\).
1461	\end{lemma}
1462	\begin{proof}
1463	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1464	  Both sides agree on each free generator \(j\): the inclusion sends it to the \(g(j)\)-th
1465	  generator, and reading off the \((p,j)\)-entry on either side gives \(M_{p, g(j)}\). Cofan
1466	  extensionality over the \(e\) generators concludes.
1467	\end{proof}

1469	\begin{lemma}[Epimorphisms of free sheaves split over an affine]
1470	  \label{lem:gr_exists_section_of_epi_free_spec}
1471	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.exists_section_of_epi_free_spec}
1472	  On an affine base \(\Spec R\), every epimorphism
1473	  \(\psi : \mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}^{\,d}\) of
1474	  free sheaves splits: there is a section
1475	  \(\Phi : \mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}^{\,r}\) with
1476	  \(\psi \circ \Phi = \mathrm{id}\).
1477	\end{lemma}
1478	\begin{proof}
1479	  Under the fully faithful additive functor \(\mathrm{Mod}_R \to (\Spec R)\text{-Mod}\)
1480	  taking a module to its associated quasi-coherent sheaf, the free sheaves correspond to the
1481	  free modules \(R^{\,r}, R^{\,d}\) and \(\psi\) to a surjection of modules. A free module is
1482	  projective, so this surjection admits a module-level section; transporting that section
1483	  back through full faithfulness produces the required \(\Phi\).
1484	\end{proof}
1485	
1486	\begin{lemma}[Matrix right inverse over \(\Spec R\)]
1487	  \label{lem:gr_exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect_spec}
1488	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect_spec}
1489	  \uses{lem:gr_exists_section_of_epi_free_spec, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp_rect,
1490	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one}
1491	  For \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(\Spec R, \mathcal{O}))\) with
1492	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) an epimorphism, the matrix admits a right inverse: there is
1493	  \(G \in \operatorname{Mat}_{r\times d}(\Gamma(\Spec R, \mathcal{O}))\) with \(M \cdot G = 1\).
1494	\end{lemma}
1495	\begin{proof}
1496	  \uses{lem:gr_exists_section_of_epi_free_spec, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp_rect,
1497	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one}
1498	  Split the epimorphism by \cref{lem:gr_exists_section_of_epi_free_spec} and read its section
1499	  off as a matrix \(G\), so that \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(G)\) is the section. The composition
1500	  law (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp_rect}) gives \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M\cdot G)\) as the
1501	  composite of section and epimorphism, which is the identity; with
1502	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one}) and injectivity
1503	  of the presentation (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}) this forces \(M \cdot G = 1\).
1504	\end{proof}
1505	
1506	\begin{lemma}[Matrix right inverse over an affine scheme]
1507	  \label{lem:gr_exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect}
1508	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect}
1509	  \uses{lem:gr_exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect_spec, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback}
1510	  For an affine scheme \(S\) and
1511	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) with
1512	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) an epimorphism, there is
1513	  \(G \in \operatorname{Mat}_{r\times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) with \(M \cdot G = 1\).
1514	\end{lemma}
1515	\begin{proof}
1516	  \uses{lem:gr_exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect_spec, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback}
1517	  Transport along the canonical isomorphism \(S \cong \Spec\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S)\). The
1518	  base-change naturality of \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\) (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback})
1519	  identifies the pulled-back homomorphism, through the free-pullback comparisons, with
1520	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\) of the transported matrix, reducing the claim to the \(\Spec\)
1521	  case (\cref{lem:gr_exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect_spec}); pulling the resulting
1522	  right inverse back along the (invertible) global-sections map gives \(G\) over \(S\).
1523	\end{proof}

1525	\begin{lemma}[Field-level minor extraction]
1526	  \label{lem:gr_exists_isUnit_submatrix}
1527	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.exists_isUnit_submatrix}
1528	  Let \(K\) be a field, \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(K)\) and
1529	  \(G \in \operatorname{Mat}_{r\times d}(K)\) with \(M \cdot G = 1\). Then some size-\(d\)
1530	  column-index set \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) gives an invertible minor: the \(d \times d\)
1531	  submatrix \(M_I\) of the \(I\)-columns (in their natural order) is a unit.
1532	\end{lemma}
1533	\begin{proof}
1534	  The right inverse makes the column map \(K^{\,r} \to K^{\,d}\) surjective, so the columns of
1535	  \(M\) span \(K^{\,d}\). A spanning family contains a basis; choosing \(d\) columns forming a
1536	  basis, the corresponding \(d \times d\) submatrix has linearly independent columns and is
1537	  therefore invertible.
1538	\end{proof}

1540	\begin{lemma}[Inverse comparison intertwines the index inclusion]
1541	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_inv_freeMap}
1542	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.pullbackFreeIso_inv_freeMap}
1543	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1544	  For a morphism \(p : W \to V\) and an index map \(g : \{1,\dots,n\} \to \{1,\dots,m\}\), the
1545	  inverse free-pullback comparisons \(Q_n, Q_m\) (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}) intertwine the
1546	  index inclusions:
1547	  \[
1548	    Q_n^{-1} \,\circ\, p^{*}\bigl(\mathrm{freeMap}(g)\bigr)
1549	      \;=\; \mathrm{freeMap}(g) \,\circ\, Q_m^{-1}.
1550	  \]
1551	\end{lemma}
1552	\begin{proof}
1553	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1554	  This is the inverse form of the forward naturality of \(\mathrm{freeMap}\) under pullback:
1555	  cancel the comparison isomorphisms \(Q_n, Q_m\) on both sides and apply the forward
1556	  intertwining identity \(p^{*}(\mathrm{freeMap}(g))\circ Q_m = Q_n \circ \mathrm{freeMap}(g)\).
1557	\end{proof}

1559	\begin{definition}[Presented matrix of a quotient along a morphism]
1560	  \label{def:gr_presentedMatrix}
1561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.presentedMatrix}
1562	  \uses{def:gr_rankQuotient, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1563	  Let \(x = \langle\mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) be a rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)
1564	  (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}), \(j : P \to T\) a morphism, and \(I\) a size-\(d\) subset such
1565	  that the pulled-back chart composite \(j^{*}(s_I \circ q)\) is an isomorphism. The
1566	  \emph{presented matrix} \(\mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j, I) \in
1567	  \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(P, \mathcal{O}_P))\) is the matrix that reads off,
1568	  entrywise via the section-extraction \(\mathrm{unitEndSection}\), the conjugated free-sheaf
1569	  homomorphism
1570	  \[
1571	    Q_r^{-1} \,\circ\, j^{*}q \,\circ\, \bigl(j^{*}(s_I \circ q)\bigr)^{-1}
1572	      \,\circ\, Q_d
1573	      \;:\; \mathcal{O}_P^{\,r} \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_P^{\,d},
1574	  \]
1575	  where \(Q_r, Q_d\) are the free-pullback comparisons (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}). It
1576	  generalises the chart matrix (the case where \(j\) is the inclusion of the chart locus
1577	  \(T_I\)) and is the vehicle of the overlap comparison.
1578	\end{definition}
1579	
1580	\begin{lemma}[The presented matrix presents]
1581	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix}
1582	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix}
1583	  \uses{def:gr_presentedMatrix, def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1584	  With the data of \cref{def:gr_presentedMatrix},
1585	  \[
1586	    \mathrm{matrixEndRect}\bigl(\mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j, I)\bigr)
1587	      \;=\; Q_r^{-1} \,\circ\, j^{*}q \,\circ\, \bigl(j^{*}(s_I \circ q)\bigr)^{-1}
1588	      \,\circ\, Q_d.
1589	  \]
1590	\end{lemma}
1591	\begin{proof}
1592	  \uses{def:gr_presentedMatrix}
1593	  Immediate from the definition: \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\) of the
1594	  \(\mathrm{unitEndSection}\)-matrix of a free-sheaf homomorphism recovers that homomorphism.
1595	\end{proof}
1596	
1597	\begin{lemma}[Minor of the presented matrix presents the \(J\)-chart composite]
1598	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor}
1599	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor}
1600	  \uses{lem:gr_freeMap_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_inv_freeMap,
1601	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix}
1602	  For a second size-\(d\) subset \(J\), the \(J\)-minor of the \(I\)-presented matrix presents
1603	  the pulled-back \(J\)-chart composite against the inverted \(I\)-chart composite:
1604	  \[
1605	    \mathrm{matrixEndRect}\bigl(\mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j, I)_{\bullet,\, J}\bigr)
1606	      \;=\; Q_d^{-1} \,\circ\, j^{*}(s_J \circ q) \,\circ\,
1607	        \bigl(j^{*}(s_I \circ q)\bigr)^{-1} \,\circ\, Q_d.
1608	  \]
1609	\end{lemma}
1610	\begin{proof}
1611	  \uses{lem:gr_freeMap_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_inv_freeMap,
1612	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix}
1613	  Column restriction (\cref{lem:gr_freeMap_matrixEndRect}) turns the \(J\)-minor into
1614	  pre-composition with the index inclusion \(s_J\); the inverse-comparison intertwiner
1615	  (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_inv_freeMap}) and the presentation identity
1616	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix}) move that inclusion across the pullback, where
1617	  \(s_J \circ q\) is exactly the \(J\)-chart composite.
1618	\end{proof}
1619	
1620	\begin{lemma}[Change of basis between chart presentations]
1621	  \label{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_changeOfBasis}
1622	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.presentedMatrix_changeOfBasis}
1623	  \uses{def:gr_presentedMatrix, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix,
1624	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp_rect,
1625	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}
1626	  Along a morphism \(j\) on which both the \(I\)- and \(J\)-chart composites pull back to
1627	  isomorphisms, the \(I\)-presented matrix is the product of its own \(J\)-minor with the
1628	  \(J\)-presented matrix -- Nitsure's overlap identity \(M^I = M^I_J \cdot M^J\):
1629	  \[
1630	    \mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j, I)
1631	      \;=\; \mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j, I)_{\bullet,\, J}
1632	        \,\cdot\, \mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j, J).
1633	  \]
1634	\end{lemma}
1635	\begin{proof}
1636	  \uses{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor,
1637	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp_rect, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}
1638	  Apply \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\) and use injectivity (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}).
1639	  By the composition law (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp_rect}) the right side becomes the
1640	  composite of the two presented homomorphisms; substituting
1641	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix} (for both factors) and
1642	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor} (for the minor), the pulled-back
1643	  \(J\)-chart composite \(j^{*}(s_J \circ q)\) cancels with its inverse, leaving
1644	  \(Q_r^{-1} \circ j^{*}q \circ (j^{*}(s_I \circ q))^{-1} \circ Q_d =
1645	  \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(\mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j, I))\).
1646	\end{proof}

1648	\begin{lemma}[Invertible presentation forces a unit matrix]
1649	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_of_isIso_matrixEndRect}
1650	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_of_isIso_matrixEndRect}
1651	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp_rect,
1652	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one}
1653	  Converse of \cref{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso}: if \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(N)\) is an
1654	  isomorphism for a square \(N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\),
1655	  then \(N\) is a unit (an invertible matrix).
1656	\end{lemma}
1657	\begin{proof}
1658	  \uses{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp_rect, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective,
1659	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one}
1660	  Read off the inverse morphism by its \(\mathrm{unitEndSection}\) matrix \(N'\), so that
1661	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(N') = \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(N)^{-1}\). The composition law
1662	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp_rect}), the identity law
1663	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one}) and injectivity (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective})
1664	  turn the two-sided inverse relation of morphisms into \(N \cdot N' = 1 = N' \cdot N\),
1665	  exhibiting \(N\) as a unit.
1666	\end{proof}
