1668	\begin{definition}
1669	
1670	[Rectangular matrix homomorphism of free sheaves]
1671	  \label{def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect}
1673	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
1674	  Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(d, r \in \mathbb{N}\), and
1675	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times r}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) a rectangular matrix
1676	  of global functions. The \emph{rectangular matrix homomorphism}
1677	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) is the morphism of free sheaves
1678	  \[
1679	    \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M) \;:\; \mathcal{O}_S^{\,r}
1680	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}
1681	  \]
1682	  whose \((k,\ell)\)-component -- \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\) an output index,
1683	  \(\ell \in \{1,\dots,r\}\) an input index -- under the presentation of
1684	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,r}\) and \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) as finite biproducts of copies of the
1685	  unit module \(\mathbf 1\), is the scalar endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{k,\ell})\)
1686	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}). It is assembled from these components by the universal property
1687	  of the two biproducts, exactly as the square \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\)
1688	  (\cref{def:gr_matrixEnd}) and the chart quotient (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}). When
1689	  \(r = d\) it specialises to \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\); and the chart quotient \(u^I\)
1690	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is by construction \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(X^I)\) of the
1691	  universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}).
1692	\end{definition}
1693	
1694	\begin{lemma}
1695	
1696	[Rectangular matrix homomorphism is natural under pullback]
1697	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback}
1698	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_pullback}
1699	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback,
1700	    lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:modules_pullbackComp, lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}
1701	  Let \(p : T \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and
1702	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), with entrywise
1703	  base-changed matrix \(p^{\sharp}M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(T,\mathcal{O}_T))\).
1704	  Write \(Q_r : p^{*}\mathcal{O}_S^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\) and
1705	  \(Q_d : p^{*}\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\) for the
1706	  free-pullback comparisons (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}) at the index sets
1707	  \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Then the pullback of
1708	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) is conjugate, through \(Q_r\) and \(Q_d\), to the
1709	  rectangular homomorphism of the base-changed matrix:
1710	  \[
1711	    p^{*}\bigl(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\bigr)
1712	      \;=\; Q_d^{-1} \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(p^{\sharp}M) \,\circ\, Q_r .
1713	  \]
1714	\end{lemma}
1715	\begin{proof}
1716	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1717	    def:modules_pullbackComp, lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}
1718	  Identical to the square case \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}, with the source and target
1719	  biproducts now indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\) respectively. The
1720	  pullback functor \(p^{*}\), a left adjoint, is additive and biproduct-preserving; through
1721	  the coproduct comparisons underlying the free-pullback isomorphisms
1722	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} it carries the biproduct presentation of
1723	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,r}\) (resp. \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\)) to that of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)
1724	  (resp. \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}\)), the reassociation of the iterated pullbacks being
1725	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. On each single \((k,\ell)\)-component the pulled-back
1726	  scalar endomorphism is, by \cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_pullback}, the comparison-conjugate of
1727	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}((p^{\sharp}M)_{k,\ell})\). Reassembling over the two biproducts, the
1728	  conjugating one-element comparisons combine into \(Q_r\) on the source and \(Q_d\) on the
1729	  target, giving \(p^{*}(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)) = Q_d^{-1}\circ
1730	  \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(p^{\sharp}M)\circ Q_r\).
1731	\end{proof}
1732	
1733	\begin{lemma}
1734	
1735	[Square-after-rectangular composition law]
1736	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}
1737	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_comp}
1738	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add,
1739	    lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
1740	  For \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\) and
1741	  \(N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), post-composing the
1742	  rectangular homomorphism with a square one realises the matrix product, with the order
1743	  reversed by the contravariance of the component indexing exactly as in
1744	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}:
1745	  \[
1746	    \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M) \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)
1747	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(N \cdot M),
1748	  \]
1749	  the composite \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)}
1750	  \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)} \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) (here
1751	  \(N \cdot M\) is the \(d \times r\) product).
1752	\end{lemma}
1753	\begin{proof}
1754	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add,
1755	    lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
1756	  The same biproduct-extensionality computation as \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}, the only
1757	  change being that the leftmost (input) biproduct is indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\) rather
1758	  than \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). The composite of two biproduct-assembled morphisms is again
1759	  biproduct-assembled, with \((k,\ell)\)-component the sum over the middle index \(m\) of the
1760	  composites of components, \(\sum_m \mathrm{scalarEnd}(N_{k,m}) \circ
1761	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{m,\ell})\). Each composite of scalar endomorphisms is the scalar
1762	  endomorphism of the product (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}) and a finite sum of scalar
1763	  endomorphisms is the scalar endomorphism of the sum (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}); hence
1764	  that component is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(\sum_m N_{k,m} M_{m,\ell}) =
1765	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}((N\cdot M)_{k,\ell})\), the \((k,\ell)\)-component of
1766	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(N\cdot M)\).
1767	\end{proof}
1768	
1769	\begin{lemma}
1770	
1771	[Adjunction transpose of the chart-overlap condition]
1772	  \label{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose}
1773	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose}
1774	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_bundleTransition, def:gr_modules_glueHom,
1775	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app,
1776	    lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1777	    def:modules_pullbackComp}
1778	  Fix size-\(d\) subsets \(I, J\) and let \(f_{IJ} : U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\),
1779	  \(t_{IJ} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\) be the overlap immersion and transition
1780	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}). The per-chart components \(u^I\) of the tautological quotient
1781	  are the adjoint transposes, along the chart immersions, of the chart quotients
1782	  \cref{def:gr_chart_quotient} (precomposed with the free-pullback comparison). Their
1783	  descent compatibility -- the hypothesis the gluing primitive \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom}
1784	  consumes, namely that the bundle transition \(g_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_bundleTransition})
1785	  intertwines the pullbacks of \(u^I\) and \(u^J\) over \(U^I_J\) -- is equivalent, under the
1786	  pullback--pushforward adjunction of the overlap immersion, to the pullback-level identity
1787	  of morphisms \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,d}\)
1788	  \[
1789	    g_{I,J} \,\circ\, f_{IJ}^{*}\,u^I
1790	      \;=\; (t_{IJ} \circ f_{JI})^{*}\,u^J,
1791	  \]
1792	  understood through the free-pullback comparisons (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}) on
1793	  source and target.
1794	\end{lemma}
1795	\begin{proof}
1796	  \uses{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app, lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib,
1797	    lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1798	  The components are produced by applying the adjunction hom-equivalence to the chart
1799	  quotients; the descent condition of \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom} is stated on those
1800	  transposes. Transposing back across the adjunction is the naturality of the conjugate
1801	  (mate) construction against the hom-equivalences, \cref{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app},
1802	  together with the compatibility of the conjugate with the iterated-pullback comparison
1803	  \cref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} reassociating the two pullbacks
1804	  \(f_{IJ}^{*}\) and \((t_{IJ}\circ f_{JI})^{*}\) via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The
1805	  free-pullback comparisons \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} re-present both sides as morphisms
1806	  of the trivial bundles \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,d}\), under
1807	  which the transposed condition becomes the displayed pullback-level identity.
1808	\end{proof}

1049	\begin{corollary}\leanok
1050	[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
1051	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
1052	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
1053	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1054	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1055	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1056	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
1057	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
1058	  \[
1059	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
1060	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
1061	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
1062	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1063	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1064	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
1065	  \]
1066	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
1067	  arguments.
1068	\end{corollary}
1069	
1070	\begin{proof}
1071	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1072	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1073	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1074	    \leanok
1075	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
1076	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
1077	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
1078	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
1079	  \[
1080	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
1081	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
1082	    \qquad
1083	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
1084	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
1085	  \]
1086	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
1087	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
1088	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
1089	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
1090	  \[
1091	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
1092	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
1093	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1094	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
1095	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
1096	  \]
1097	  is an isomorphism; and, reading the same lemma through the braiding of the
1098	  presheaf monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) so as
1099	  to whisker the unit \(\eta_{b \otimes_p c}\) on the left of \(a\), the
1100	  morphism
1101	  \[
1102	    (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#} :
1103	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)\bigr)^{\#}
1104	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1105	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#}
1106	    = \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
1107	  \]
1108	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the
1109	  sheafification of the presheaf associator
1110	  \[
1111	    \alpha^p_{a,b,c} :
1112	    (a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c
1113	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1114	    a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)
1115	  \]
1116	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), an isomorphism because
1117	  sheafification is a functor and \(\alpha^p\) is one. The associator is the
1118	  composite
1119	  \[
1120	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}}
1121	    = (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#}
1122	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
1123	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
1124	  \]
1125	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
1126	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
1127	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
1128	\end{proof}
1129	
1130	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1131	[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
1132	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1133	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
1134	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
1135	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
1136	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
1137	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
1138	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1139	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
1140	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1141	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
1142	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
1143	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
1144	  %  (formulation omitted)."
1145	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
1146	  power isomorphisms).}
1147	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
1148	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1149	  \[
1150	    \mu_{m,m'} :
1151	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1152	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
1153	  \]
1154	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
1155	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
1156	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
1157	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1158	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
1159	\end{lemma}
1160	
1161	\begin{proof}
1162	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
1163	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
1164	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1165	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1166	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1167	    \leanok
1168	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
1169	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
1170	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
1171	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
1172	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
1173	
1174	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
1175	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
1176	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
1177	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
1178	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
1179	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
1180	  sheaf tensor products
1181	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
1182	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
1183	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
1184	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
1185	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
1186	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
1187	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
1188	  of the tensor product is never used.
1189	
1190	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
1191	  \(m = 0\),
1192	  \[
1193	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1194	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1195	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1196	  \]
1197	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
1198	  recursive definition
1199	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
1200	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
1201	  \[
1202	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1203	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
1204	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1205	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1206	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
1207	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1208	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
1209	  \]
1210	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
1211	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
1212	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
1213	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
1214	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
1215	  \[
1216	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
1217	      \mathcal{L}
1218	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
1219	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
1220	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
1221	  \]
1222	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
1223	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
1224	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
1225	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
1226	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
1227	
1228	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
1229	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
1230	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
1231	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
1232	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
1233	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
1234	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
1235	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
1236	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1237	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
1238	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
1239	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
1240	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
1241	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
1242	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
1243	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
1244	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
1245	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
1246	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
1247	\end{proof}
1248	
1249	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
1250	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
1251	
1252	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
1253	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
1254	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
1255	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
1256	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
1257	global sections.
1258	
1259	\begin{definition}\leanok
1260	[Section multiplication]
1261	  \label{def:sectionMul}
1262	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
1263	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1264	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1265	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
1266	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
1267	  \[
1268	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
1269	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
1270	    \;\longrightarrow\;
1271	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
1272	  \]
1273	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
1274	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
1275	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
1276	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
1277	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
1278	  the sheafification unit
1279	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
1280	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
1281	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
1282	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
1283	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
1284	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
1285	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
1286	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
1287	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
1288	\end{definition}
1289	
1290	The graded-monoid axioms below are not raw equalities between sections of two
1291	different tensor powers --- on \(\mathbb{N}\) the indices \((i+j)+k\) and
1292	\(i+(j+k)\), or \(0+n\) and \(n\), are equal but not definitionally so, so the two
1293	sides a priori inhabit distinct section modules. Following
1294	\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorPower.Basic}, we phrase each coherence as an
1295	honest equality in a single section module, obtained by transporting one side
1296	along an index-equality isomorphism, and provide a bridge that repackages such a
1297	transported equality as an equality of dependent pairs.
1298	
1299	\begin{definition}[Index-equality transport of section components]
1300	  \label{def:sectionsCast}
1301	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsCast}
1302	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow}
1303	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be an invertible sheaf on \(X\) and let \(h : i = j\) be an
1304	  equality of natural numbers. Applying the global-sections functor
1305	  \(\Gamma(X,-)\) to the canonical isomorphism
1306	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes i} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes j}\) induced by
1307	  \(h\) under the tensor-power functor (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) yields the
1308	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linear isomorphism
1309	  \[
1310	    \mathrm{sectionsCast}(h) :
1311	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes i}) \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1312	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes j}),
1313	  \]
1314	  the \emph{section-component transport} along \(h\). It is the section-level
1315	  analogue of the tensor-power index cast \(\mathrm{TensorPower.cast}\) of
1316	  \texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorPower.Basic}, which transports
1317	  \(\bigotimes^{i} M\) to \(\bigotimes^{j} M\) along \(i = j\). Because the
1318	  isomorphism induced by the reflexive equality is the identity and
1319	  \(\Gamma(X,-)\) preserves identities, the transport along a reflexive index
1320	  equality is the identity map (the reflexivity simplification
1321	  \(\mathrm{sectionsCast\_refl}\), \cref{lem:sectionsCast_refl}).
1322	\end{definition}
1323	
1324	\begin{lemma}[Reflexivity of the section transport]
1325	  \label{lem:sectionsCast_refl}
1326	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsCast_refl}
1327	  \uses{def:sectionsCast}
1328	  For any \(i\), the transport along the reflexive equality \(i = i\)
1329	  (\cref{def:sectionsCast}) equals the identity automorphism of
1330	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes i})\). This is the section-level counterpart of
1331	  \(\mathrm{TensorPower.cast\_refl}\), and serves as a simplification lemma
1332	  collapsing the transport along a reflexive index equality.
1333	\end{lemma}
1334	
1335	\begin{proof}
1336	  The canonical isomorphism
1337	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes i} \cong \mathcal{L}^{\otimes i}\) induced by the
1338	  reflexive equality is the identity, and the global-sections functor carries
1339	  identities to identities.
1340	\end{proof}
1341	
1342	\begin{lemma}[Cast-mediated equality in the graded monoid]
1343	  \label{lem:gradedMonoid_eq_of_cast}
1344	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.gradedMonoid_eq_of_cast}
1345	  \uses{def:sectionsCast, lem:sectionsCast_refl}
1346	  Write the carrier of the graded monoid as the dependent sum
1347	  \(\coprod_{n} \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\), whose elements are pairs
1348	  \((n, s)\) with \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\). Let
1349	  \(a = (i, x)\) and \(b = (j, y)\) be two such pairs. If \(h : i = j\) and the
1350	  transported component agrees, \(\mathrm{sectionsCast}(h)\,x = y\), then
1351	  \(a = b\). That is, a component-level equality mediated by the index transport
1352	  \cref{def:sectionsCast} repackages into a genuine equality of dependent pairs.
1353	  This is the section-level analogue of the bridge
1354	  \(\mathrm{gradedMonoid\_eq\_of\_cast}\) of
1355	  \texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorPower.Basic} (line~123 there).
1356	\end{lemma}
1357	
1358	\begin{proof}
1359	  \uses{lem:sectionsCast_refl}
1360	  Substituting \(j = i\) by means of \(h\) reduces the transport to the identity
1361	  (\cref{lem:sectionsCast_refl}), so the hypothesis becomes \(x = y\); the two
1362	  dependent pairs \((i, x)\) and \((i, y)\) then coincide.
1363	\end{proof}
1364	
1365	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
1366	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
1367	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_one_mul,
1368	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_mul_one,
1369	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_mul_assoc,
1370	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_mul_comm}
1371	  \uses{def:sectionMul, def:sectionsCast, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib,
1372	    lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1373	  Write \(a \cdot b\) for the degreewise section multiplication on tensor powers:
1374	  for \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes i})\) and
1375	  \(b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes j})\) it is the elementary section
1376	  multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the tensor-power comparison
1377	  isomorphism \(\mu_{i,j}\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), landing in
1378	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (i+j)})\); and let \(1\) denote the unit of
1379	  degree \(0\), the image of \(1 \in \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) under the
1380	  identification \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0})\)
1381	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Mirroring
1382	  \(\mathrm{TensorPower.\{one\_mul, mul\_one, mul\_assoc\}}\) together with the
1383	  commutativity lemma of \texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorPower.Basic}, the
1384	  multiplication satisfies the following four component-level identities. Each is
1385	  an honest equality in a single section module, obtained by transporting one side
1386	  along the relevant index equality via \cref{def:sectionsCast}; the subscript on
1387	  \(\mathrm{sectionsCast}\) records that index equality.
1388	  \begin{itemize}
1389	    \item \emph{Left unitality.} For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\),
1390	      \[
1391	        \mathrm{sectionsCast}_{\,0+n=n}\,(1 \cdot a) = a .
1392	      \]
1393	    \item \emph{Right unitality.} For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\),
1394	      \[
1395	        \mathrm{sectionsCast}_{\,n+0=n}\,(a \cdot 1) = a .
1396	      \]
1397	    \item \emph{Associativity.} For
1398	      \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n_a})\),
1399	      \(b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n_b})\),
1400	      \(c \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n_c})\),
1401	      \[
1402	        \mathrm{sectionsCast}_{\,(n_a+n_b)+n_c = n_a+(n_b+n_c)}\,
1403	          ((a \cdot b) \cdot c) = a \cdot (b \cdot c) .
1404	      \]
1405	    \item \emph{Commutativity.} For
1406	      \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n_a})\) and
1407	      \(b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n_b})\),
1408	      \[
1409	        \mathrm{sectionsCast}_{\,n_a+n_b = n_b+n_a}\,(a \cdot b) = b \cdot a .
1410	      \]
1411	  \end{itemize}
1412	  The transport is indispensable: the indices on the two sides of each identity
1413	  are equal but not definitionally so, hence a priori live in distinct section
1414	  modules, and \cref{def:sectionsCast} moves one side into the other's module to
1415	  produce a genuine equality there.
1416	\end{lemma}
1417	
1418	\begin{proof}
1419	  \uses{def:sectionMul, def:sectionsCast, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1420	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add,
1421	    cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
1422	  All four identities reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
1423	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
1424	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator, unitors and
1425	  symmetry act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
1426	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
1427	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\),
1428	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\) and
1429	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \tau \otimes \sigma\). The sheafification unit
1430	  \(\eta\) is a natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator,
1431	  unitors and symmetry (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying
1432	  \(\Gamma(X,-)\) to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level
1433	  identities to the section multiplications, and the index transport
1434	  \cref{def:sectionsCast} is precisely the image under \(\Gamma(X,-)\) of the
1435	  tensor-power comparison \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}) along which the
1436	  two sides are matched.
1437	\end{proof}
1438	
1439	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
1440	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
1441	
1442	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
1443	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
1444	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
1445	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
1446	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
1447	
1448	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
1449	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1450	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
1451	  \mathlibok
1452	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
1453	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1454	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
1455	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
1456	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
1457	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
1458	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
1459	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
1460	  \(1 \in A_0\).
1461	\end{lemma}
1462	
1463	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
1464	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
1465	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
1466	  \mathlibok
1467	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
1468	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
1469	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
1470	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1471	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
1472	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
1473	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
1474	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
1475	  extension of the graded action.
1476	\end{lemma}
1477	
1478	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
1479	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1480	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1481	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
1482	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, def:sectionsCast, lem:gradedMonoid_eq_of_cast,
1483	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
1484	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
1485	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
1486	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
1487	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1488	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
1489	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
1490	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
1491	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
1492	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in

1496	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
1497	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
1498	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
1499	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
1500	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
1501	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
1502	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
1503	  multiplication is the composite
1504	  \[
1505	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
1506	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
1507	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
1508	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
1509	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
1510	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
1511	  \]
1512	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
1513	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
1514	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
1515	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
1516	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
1517	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
1518	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
1519	\end{lemma}
1520	
1521	\begin{proof}
1522	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent, def:sectionsCast,
1523	    lem:gradedMonoid_eq_of_cast, def:sectionMul,
1524	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1525	  The structure is assembled field-for-field as \(\mathrm{TensorPower}\) builds its
1526	  graded semiring in \texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorPower.Basic}. The
1527	  degreewise multiplication \(\Gamma(\mu_{m,m'}) \circ (\,\cdot\,)\) of the
1528	  statement and the degree-\(0\) unit \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0})\)
1529	  provide the graded multiplication and unit. Their graded-monoid axioms --- left
1530	  and right unitality and associativity --- are obtained from the corresponding
1531	  component-level identities of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} by passing each
1532	  through the bridge \cref{lem:gradedMonoid_eq_of_cast}, which converts a
1533	  transport-mediated equality (\cref{def:sectionsCast}) into the required equality
1534	  of dependent pairs; the graded power operation is the default iterated product
1535	  and contributes no further data. This yields the graded-monoid layer.
1536	
1537	  The remaining semiring axioms are the bilinearity (left and right distributivity
1538	  and annihilation by \(0\)) of the graded multiplication and the behaviour of the
1539	  natural-number coercion. Bilinearity is immediate: the degreewise multiplication
1540	  \(\Gamma(\mu_{m,m'}) \circ (\,\cdot\,)\) is \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear,
1541	  being the composite of the bilinear section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul})
1542	  with the linear comparison \(\Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\)
1543	  (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), so each side vanishes on \(0\) and distributes
1544	  over sums. The natural-number coercion sends \(n\) to \(n \cdot 1\), the
1545	  \(n\)-fold sum of the degree-\(0\) unit. This completes the graded-semiring
1546	  layer.
1547	
1548	  Finally graded commutativity is the commutativity identity of
1549	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent}, again passed through
1550	  \cref{lem:gradedMonoid_eq_of_cast}. These are precisely the data of a
1551	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})\),
1552	  so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} endows
1553	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its commutative-semiring
1554	  structure.
1555	\end{proof}

1609	  \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) is assembled field-for-field as the graded-action precedent

1611	  indices given by ordinary addition on \(\mathbb{N}\) (so the degree-\((m,m')\)
1612	  action lands in degree \(m+m'\)). Its sigma-type axioms --- unitality
1613	  \(1 \cdot x = x\) and compatibility

1904	\section{The functor of points}
1905	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
1906	
1907	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
1908	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
1909	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
1910	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
1911	identities that make that action functorial.
1912	
1913	\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
1914	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
1915	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
1916	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
1917	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
1918	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
1919	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
1920	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
1921	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
1922	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
1923	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1924	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1925	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
1926	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
1927	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
1928	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
1929	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
1930	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
1931	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
1932	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
1933	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
1934	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
1935	  functor's value at \(T\).
1936	
1937	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
1938	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
1939	  \[
1940	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
1941	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
1942	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1943	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
1944	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
1945	  \]
1946	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
1947	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
1948	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
1949	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
1950	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
1951	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
1952	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
1953	  map of equivalence classes.
1954	\end{definition}

2081	\begin{definition}
2082	
2083	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
2084	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
2085	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
2086	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
2087	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
2088	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
2089	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
2090	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
2091	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2092	  % "represents the
2093	  %  contravariant functor
2094	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
2095	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
2096	  %  set of all equivalence classes
2097	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
2098	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
2099	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
2100	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
2101	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
2102	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
2103	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
2104	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
2105	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
2106	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
2107	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
2108	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
2109	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
2110	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
2111	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
2112	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
2113	  \[
2114	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
2115	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
2116	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
2117	  \]
2118	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
2119	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
2120	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
2121	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
2122	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
2123	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
2124	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
2125	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
2126	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
2127	\end{definition}
2128	
2129	\section{The universal property}
2130	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
2131	
2132	The representability theorem \cref{thm:grassmannian_universal_property} has two halves:
2133	the forward map \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u\rangle\)
2134	and its inverse, which manufactures a morphism \(T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) out of a rank-\(d\)
2135	quotient \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\). The forward map needs the tautological
2136	quotient to actually be a quotient -- that \(u\) is an epimorphism
2137	(\cref{lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi}). The inverse is built Zariski-locally and glued: for
2138	each size-\(d\) subset \(I\) one isolates the open locus \(T_I \subseteq T\) where the
2139	\(I\)-indexed columns of \(q\) form a basis of \(\mathcal{F}\), classifies \(T_I \to U^I\)
2140	there, and glues over the cover \(\{T_I\}\). The blocks of this section supply the four
2141	ingredients of that inverse: the iso-locus of a sheaf morphism (\cref{def:isoLocus}) and
2142	the fact that a morphism becomes invertible after restriction to its iso-locus
2143	(\cref{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}); the chart locus \(T_I\) (\cref{def:chartLocus})
2144	and the proof that the chart loci cover \(T\) (\cref{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}); and the
2145	glued classifying morphism itself (\cref{def:grPointOfRankQuotient}).
2146	
2147	\begin{lemma}[The tautological quotient is an epimorphism]
2148	  \label{lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi}
2149	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient_epi}
2150	  \uses{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi, def:tautological_quotient}
2151	  The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
2152	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}) is an epimorphism of
2153	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-modules.
2154	\end{lemma}
2155	\begin{proof}
2156	  \uses{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi, def:tautological_quotient}
2157	  Being an epimorphism of sheaves of modules is local on the base: a morphism is epi
2158	  precisely when its restriction to every member of an open cover is epi, since the
2159	  cokernel is computed chartwise and a sheaf vanishing on a cover vanishes. The charts
2160	  \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and over \(U^I\) the tautological quotient,
2161	  transported through the trivialisation \(\mathcal{U}|_{U^I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
2162	  of \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}, is the chart quotient \(u^I :
2163	  \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}).
2164	  Each \(u^I\) is a split epimorphism: its \(I\)-minor is the identity \(1_{d\times d}\),
2165	  so the coordinate inclusion \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow
2166	  \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) on the \(I\)-columns is a section of \(u^I\), whence \(u^I\) is
2167	  epi (\cref{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}). As \(u^I\) is epi on every chart, \(u\) is epi.
2168	\end{proof}
2169	
2170	\begin{definition}[The universal quotient sheaf restricts to the free sheaf on a chart]
2171	  \label{def:gr_universalQuotient_restrictionIso}
2172	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient_restrictionIso}
2173	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:glueRestrictionIso,
2174	    def:gr_bundleTransitionData, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_mul}
2175	  For each chart index \(I\), the restriction of the universal quotient sheaf
2176	  \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) to the \(I\)-chart along the
2177	  glue-data immersion \(\iota_I : U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is canonically isomorphic to
2178	  the free rank-\(d\) sheaf,
2179	  \[
2180	    \phi_I \;:\; \iota_I^{*}\mathcal{U}
2181	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}.
2182	  \]
2183	  It is the instantiation, at the Grassmannian glue data (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and
2184	  the bundle-transition cocycle \((g_{I,J})\) (\cref{def:gr_bundleTransitionData},
2185	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id}, \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_mul}), of the descent
2186	  restriction isomorphism (\cref{def:glueRestrictionIso}); its underlying morphism is the
2187	  adjoint transpose of the \(I\)-th descent-equaliser projection, an isomorphism because
2188	  the descent comparison morphism is invertible.
2189	\end{definition}

2191	\begin{lemma}[The universal quotient sheaf is locally free of rank \(d\)]
2192	  \label{lem:gr_universalQuotient_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
2193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
2194	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_universalQuotient_restrictionIso,
2195	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp,
2196	    lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
2197	  The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) is
2198	  locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}).
2199	\end{lemma}
2200	\begin{proof}
2201	  \uses{def:gr_universalQuotient_restrictionIso, def:modules_pullbackComp,
2202	    lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
2203	  The chart images \(\{\iota_I(U^I)\}\) form an open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), since
2204	  the glue-data immersions are jointly surjective (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). Over each
2205	  member, factor the immersion as \(\iota_I = e_I \circ j_I\) through the isomorphism
2206	  \(e_I : U^I \xrightarrow{\sim} \iota_I(U^I)\) onto its open image followed by the open
2207	  inclusion \(j_I\); transporting the chart restriction isomorphism
2208	  (\cref{def:gr_universalQuotient_restrictionIso}) across this factorisation -- inverting
2209	  the parametrisation \(e_I\) through the pullback pseudofunctor comparison
2210	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and the free-pullback comparison
2211	  (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}) -- exhibits the restriction
2212	  \(j_I^{*}\mathcal{U} \cong \mathcal{O}_{\iota_I(U^I)}^{\,d}\) as free of rank \(d\). As
2213	  the \(\iota_I(U^I)\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally free of
2214	  rank \(d\).
2215	\end{proof}

2217	\begin{definition}[The tautological point as a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2218	  \label{def:gr_tautologicalRankQuotient}

2233	\begin{definition}[Iso-locus of a morphism of sheaves of modules]
2234	  \label{def:isoLocus}
2235	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isoLocus}
2236	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) a morphism of sheaves
2237	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The \emph{iso-locus} \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi) \subseteq X\)
2238	  is the open subscheme
2239	  \[
2240	    \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)
2241	      \;=\; \bigsqcup\,\bigl\{\, U \subseteq X \text{ open} \;:\;
2242	        \varphi|_U := (\iota_U^{*}\varphi) \text{ is an isomorphism} \,\bigr\},
2243	  \]
2244	  the supremum (union) of all opens \(U\) over which the pullback \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) of
2245	  \(\varphi\) to the open subscheme \(U\) is an isomorphism of sheaves of
2246	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules. Equivalently, a point \(t \in X\) lies in
2247	  \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) iff \(\varphi\) restricts to an isomorphism over some open
2248	  neighbourhood of \(t\). It is the largest open on which \(\varphi\) is invertible; Mathlib
2249	  has no iso-locus primitive for morphisms of sheaves of modules, so it is set up directly
2250	  as this supremum.
2251	\end{definition}
2252	
2253	\begin{lemma}[Stalk-wise iso criterion]
2254	  \label{lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
2255	  \lean{TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso}
2256	  \mathlibok
2257	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
2258	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves on a topological space \(X\) valued in a
2259	  concrete category whose forgetful functor reflects isomorphisms and preserves limits and
2260	  filtered colimits, and let \(f : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}\) be a morphism. If the
2261	  induced map on stalks \(f_t : \mathcal{F}_t \to \mathcal{G}_t\) is an isomorphism for every
2262	  point \(t \in X\), then \(f\) is an isomorphism.
2263	\end{lemma}
2264	
2265	\begin{lemma}[A morphism is invertible over its iso-locus]
2266	  \label{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
2267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
2268	  \uses{def:isoLocus, lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
2269	  For a morphism \(\varphi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of
2270	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the pullback of \(\varphi\) to the iso-locus
2271	  (\cref{def:isoLocus}),
2272	  \[
2273	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\varphi
2274	      \;:\;
2275	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\mathcal{M}
2276	      \;\longrightarrow\;
2277	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\mathcal{N},
2278	  \]
2279	  is an isomorphism.
2280	\end{lemma}
2281	\begin{proof}
2282	  \uses{def:isoLocus, lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
2283	  Being an isomorphism is a stalk-local property: by the stalk-wise criterion
2284	  (\cref{lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}) it suffices to show that the pulled-back
2285	  morphism induces an isomorphism on the stalk at every point \(t \in
2286	  \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\). By the definition of the iso-locus as a union of opens on
2287	  which \(\varphi\) restricts to an isomorphism (\cref{def:isoLocus}), each such \(t\) has an
2288	  open neighbourhood \(U \subseteq \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) with \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) an
2289	  isomorphism; restriction of an isomorphism is an isomorphism, and pullback commutes with
2290	  taking stalks, so the stalk of the morphism at \(t\) coincides with the stalk of
2291	  \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) at \(t\), which is invertible. Hence every stalk map is an
2292	  isomorphism, and the criterion concludes that the global pullback over
2293	  \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism.
2294	\end{proof}

2296	\begin{definition}[Chart composite of a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2297	  \label{def:gr_chartComposite}
2298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartComposite}
2299	  \uses{def:gr_rankQuotient}
2300	  Let \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) be a rank-\(d\) quotient of
2301	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}) and \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) a
2302	  size-\(d\) subset, enumerated in increasing order by the order isomorphism
2303	  \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\). The \emph{chart composite} is the morphism of
2304	  sheaves of modules
2305	  \[
2306	    s_I \circ q \;:\; \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
2307	      \overset{\ s_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
2308	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
2309	  \]
2310	  the quotient \(q\) precomposed with the coordinate inclusion \(s_I\) of the
2311	  \(I\)-indexed factors of the trivial bundle. Over the locus where it is invertible the
2312	  \(I\)-columns of \(q\) form a basis of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2313	\end{definition}
2314	
2315	\begin{definition}[Chart locus of a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2316	  \label{def:chartLocus}
2317	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartLocus}
2318	  \uses{def:isoLocus, def:gr_chartComposite, def:gr_rankQuotient}
2319	  Let \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) be a rank-\(d\) quotient of
2320	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\) on a scheme \(T\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}) and \(I \subseteq
2321	  \{1,\dots,r\}\) a size-\(d\) subset. The \emph{chart composite} is the morphism of sheaves
2322	  of modules
2323	  \[
2324	    s_I \circ q
2325	      \;:\;
2326	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
2327	      \overset{\ s_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
2328	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
2329	  \]
2330	  obtained by precomposing the quotient \(q\) with the inclusion \(s_I\) of the
2331	  \(I\)-indexed coordinates of the trivial bundle. The \emph{chart locus}
2332	  \(T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I) \subseteq T\) is the iso-locus
2333	  (\cref{def:isoLocus}) of this chart composite:
2334	  \[
2335	    T_I \;=\; \mathrm{isoLocus}(s_I \circ q),
2336	  \]
2337	  the largest open of \(T\) over which the \(I\)-columns of \(q\) form a basis of
2338	  \(\mathcal{F}\) -- i.e.\ over which \(s_I \circ q\) is an isomorphism. Over \(T_I\) the
2339	  quotient \(q\) is therefore presented, after trivialising \(\mathcal{F}\) by
2340	  \((s_I \circ q)^{-1}\), by a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-minor is the identity,
2341	  exactly the shape of the universal matrix \(X^I\) realising the chart quotient \(u^I\)
2342	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
2343	\end{definition}

2345	\begin{lemma}[The chart loci cover \(T\)]
2346	  \label{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}
2347	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartLocus_isOpenCover}
2348	  \uses{def:chartLocus, def:gr_rankQuotient}
2349	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
2350	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
2351	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L823-L828).
2352	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2353	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
2354	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
2355	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
2356	  %  Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
2357	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
2358	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
2359	  %  on $U^I_J$."
2360	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2361	  For a rank-\(d\) quotient \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)
2362	  on \(T\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}), the family of chart loci
2363	  \(\{\,T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I)\,\}_{\#(I) = d}\) (\cref{def:chartLocus}) is an open
2364	  cover of \(T\): \(\bigcup_{\#(I)=d} T_I = T\).
2365	\end{lemma}
2366	\begin{proof}
2367	  \uses{def:chartLocus, def:gr_rankQuotient, def:isoLocus, def:gr_matrixEndRect,
2368	    lem:gr_exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_exists_isUnit_submatrix}
2369	  Each \(T_I\) is open, being an iso-locus (\cref{def:isoLocus}). It remains to show the
2370	  \(T_I\) cover \(T\), i.e.\ that every point \(t \in T\) lies in some \(T_I\). Fix \(t\) and
2371	  choose an \emph{affine} open \(W \ni t\) over which \(\mathcal{F}\) is trivialised,
2372	  \(\mathcal{F}|_W \cong \mathcal{O}_W^{\,d}\) (such \(W\) exists because \(\mathcal{F}\) is
2373	  locally free of rank \(d\)); over \(W\) the quotient \(q\) is presented by a \(d \times r\)
2374	  matrix \(M\) of sections of \(\mathcal{O}_W\), i.e.\ \(q|_W\) is conjugate to
2375	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) (\cref{def:gr_matrixEndRect}).
2376	
2377	  \emph{Global splitting over \(W\).} Because \(q\) is an epimorphism
2378	  (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}), so is the presenting homomorphism
2379	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) of free sheaves on the affine \(W\). Over an affine base such
2380	  an epimorphism of free sheaves splits -- free modules are projective, so the epimorphism
2381	  admits a section -- and reading the section off as a matrix yields a right inverse
2382	  \(G \in \operatorname{Mat}_{r\times d}(\Gamma(W,\mathcal{O}_W))\) with \(M \cdot G = 1\)
2383	  (\cref{lem:gr_exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect}). Thus \(q\) is globally split over
2384	  \(W\), with no recourse to stalks or Nakayama propagation.
2385	
2386	  \emph{Choosing the index set at \(t\).} Reduce the identity \(M \cdot G = 1\) modulo the
2387	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(t\): writing \(\kappa(t)\) for the residue field, the
2388	  images \(\bar M, \bar G\) over \(\kappa(t)\) still satisfy \(\bar M \cdot \bar G = 1\), so
2389	  \(\bar M\) is a right-invertible \(d \times r\) matrix over the field \(\kappa(t)\). A
2390	  right-invertible matrix over a field has an invertible \(d\)-column minor: its columns span
2391	  \(\kappa(t)^{d}\), a spanning family contains a basis, and the corresponding \(d\) columns
2392	  form an invertible \(d \times d\) submatrix (\cref{lem:gr_exists_isUnit_submatrix}). Pick
2393	  such a size-\(d\) index set \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\).
2394	
2395	  \emph{Cutting out the basic open.} Let \(P_I = \det(M_I)\) be the determinant of the
2396	  \(I\)-indexed \(d \times d\) minor of \(M\). By the previous step its residue at \(t\) is
2397	  nonzero, so \(P_I\) is a unit at \(t\); hence \(t\) lies in the basic open
2398	  \(W_{P_I} = \{P_I \ne 0\} \subseteq W\) where \(P_I\) is invertible. On \(W_{P_I}\) the
2399	  minor matrix \(M_I\) is invertible, so the chart composite \(s_I \circ q\) -- conjugate
2400	  there to \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M_I)\) -- restricts to an isomorphism, i.e.\
2401	  \(W_{P_I} \subseteq \mathrm{isoLocus}(s_I \circ q) = T_I\) (\cref{def:chartLocus}).
2402	  Therefore \(t \in T_I\), and the chart loci cover \(T\).
2403	\end{proof}
2404	
2405	\begin{definition}[Chart matrix of a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2406	  \label{def:gr_chartMatrix}
2407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartMatrix}
2408	  \uses{def:gr_chartComposite, def:chartLocus, lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map,
2409	    lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:gr_presentedMatrix}
2410	  Over the chart locus \(T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I)\) (\cref{def:chartLocus}) the chart
2411	  composite \(s_I \circ q\) pulls back to an isomorphism
2412	  (\cref{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}). Trivialising \(\mathcal{F}|_{T_I}\) by its
2413	  inverse presents the restricted quotient as the conjugated free-sheaf homomorphism
2414	  \[
2415	    Q_r^{-1} \circ \iota_{T_I}^{*}q \circ (\iota_{T_I}^{*}(s_I \circ q))^{-1} \circ Q_d
2416	      \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d},
2417	  \]
2418	  with \(Q_r, Q_d\) the free-pullback comparisons (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}). The
2419	  \emph{chart matrix} \(M^I = \mathrm{chartMatrix}(x, I) \in
2420	  \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I}))\) reads off this
2421	  homomorphism entrywise through the section-extraction \(\mathrm{unitEndSection}\). It is
2422	  the special case of the presented matrix (\cref{def:gr_presentedMatrix}) along the
2423	  chart-locus inclusion; its \(I\)-minor is the identity \(1_{d\times d}\).
2424	\end{definition}
2425	
2426	\begin{definition}[Chart morphism of a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2427	  \label{def:gr_chartMorphism}
2428	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartMorphism}
2429	  \uses{def:gr_chartMatrix, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix}
2430	  The \emph{chart morphism} \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\) into the affine chart
2431	  \(U^I = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is the morphism classified,
2432	  through the \(\Gamma\)--\(\Spec\) adjunction, by the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism
2433	  \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending each variable
2434	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) to the
2435	  corresponding entry of the chart matrix \(M^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chartMatrix}). By
2436	  construction \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\), and hence \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q|_{T_I}\) in the
2437	  \(I\)-trivialisation.
2438	\end{definition}
2439	
2440	\subsection{Overlap compatibility of the chart morphisms}
2441	
2442	On the intersection \(P = T_I \times_T T_J\) of two chart loci both chart composites pull
2443	back to isomorphisms, so the pulled-back quotient is presented by both chart matrices. The
2444	blocks below assemble the transports comparing the two presentations and proving the two
2445	chart morphisms agree into the glued scheme: invertibility along a composite
2446	(\cref{lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp}), the presented matrix along a composite
2447	(\cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_comp}) and under an equality of base morphisms
2448	(\cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_congr}), the chart matrix as a presented matrix
2449	(\cref{lem:gr_chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix}), its own minor
2450	(\cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_submatrix_self}), the realisation
2451	\(\mathrm{aeval}(M^I)(X^I) = M^I\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix}), the
2452	image-matrix transport (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_ringHom}), chart-morphism naturality
2453	(\cref{lem:gr_comp_chartMorphism}), the glue-condition identity
2454	(\cref{lem:gr_chart_point_eq}), and finally the overlap compatibility itself
2455	(\cref{lem:gr_chartMorphism_glue_compat}).
2456	
2457	\begin{lemma}[Pullback-invertibility persists along precomposition]
2458	  \label{lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp}
2459	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isIso_pullback_map_comp}
2460	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp}
2461	  For composable scheme morphisms \(p : W \to V\), \(a : V \to X\) and a morphism
2462	  \(c : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules: if the
2463	  pullback \(a^{*}c\) is an isomorphism, so is \((p \circ a)^{*}c\).
2464	\end{lemma}
2465	\begin{proof}
2466	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp}
2467	  Functoriality of pullback makes \(p^{*}(a^{*}c)\) an isomorphism; the pseudofunctor
2468	  comparison \((p \circ a)^{*} \cong p^{*}a^{*}\) (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp})
2469	  identifies \((p \circ a)^{*}c\) with \(p^{*}(a^{*}c)\), which is invertible.
2470	\end{proof}
2471	
2472	\begin{lemma}[The presented matrix depends only on the base morphism]
2473	  \label{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_congr}
2474	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.presentedMatrix_congr}
2475	  \uses{def:gr_presentedMatrix}
2476	  If \(j = j'\) as morphisms \(P \to T\), then the presented matrices along \(j\) and \(j'\)
2477	  agree, \(\mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j, I) = \mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j', I)\); the
2478	  choice of invertibility witness for the pulled-back chart composite is irrelevant.
2479	\end{lemma}
2480	\begin{proof}
2481	  \uses{def:gr_presentedMatrix}
2482	  Substitute the equality \(j = j'\); the invertibility hypotheses are propositions, hence
2483	  proof-irrelevant, and the two sides become definitionally equal.
2484	\end{proof}
2485	
2486	\begin{lemma}[The chart matrix is the presented matrix along the locus inclusion]
2487	  \label{lem:gr_chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix}
2488	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix}
2489	  \uses{def:gr_chartMatrix, def:gr_presentedMatrix}
2490	  The chart matrix \(M^I = \mathrm{chartMatrix}(x, I)\) (\cref{def:gr_chartMatrix}) equals
2491	  the presented matrix (\cref{def:gr_presentedMatrix}) of \(x\) along the chart-locus
2492	  inclusion \(\iota_{T_I} : T_I \hookrightarrow T\):
2493	  \(\mathrm{chartMatrix}(x, I) = \mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, \iota_{T_I}, I)\).
2494	\end{lemma}
2495	\begin{proof}
2496	  \uses{def:gr_chartMatrix, def:gr_presentedMatrix}
2497	  Both sides are the same \(\mathrm{unitEndSection}\)-matrix of the same conjugated
2498	  free-sheaf homomorphism; the equality is definitional.
2499	\end{proof}
2500	
2501	\begin{lemma}[Transport of the presented matrix along a composite]
2502	  \label{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_comp}
2503	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.presentedMatrix_comp}
2504	  \uses{def:gr_presentedMatrix, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix,
2505	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}
2506	  Along composable \(p : W \to V\), \(a : V \to T\) on which the \(I\)-chart composite
2507	  pulls back to isomorphisms, the presented matrix after pulling back along \(p \circ a\)
2508	  is the entrywise restriction along \(p\) of the presented matrix after pulling back
2509	  along \(a\):
2510	  \[
2511	    \mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, p \circ a, I)
2512	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, a, I)\bigr)\big|_{p},
2513	  \]
2514	  the matrix obtained by applying the restriction ring map \(\Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_V) \to
2515	  \Gamma(W, \mathcal{O}_W)\) of \(p\) to every entry.
2516	\end{lemma}
2517	\begin{proof}
2518	  \uses{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}
2519	  By injectivity of the rectangular matrix presentation
2520	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}) it suffices to compare after applying
2521	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\). The presentation identity
2522	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix}) expresses both sides as conjugated
2523	  pulled-back homomorphisms; the conjugation distributes over the composite \(p \circ a\)
2524	  through the pullback pseudofunctor comparisons, and the entrywise restriction along
2525	  \(p\) is exactly the pullback along \(p\) of the \(a\)-level homomorphism. Comparing the
2526	  two yields the claimed equality.
2527	\end{proof}
2528	
2529	\begin{lemma}[The own-minor of the presented matrix is the identity]
2530	  \label{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_submatrix_self}
2531	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.presentedMatrix_submatrix_self}
2532	  \uses{def:gr_presentedMatrix, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor,
2533	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}
2534	  Along any \(j\) on which the \(I\)-chart composite pulls back to an isomorphism, the
2535	  \(I\)-minor of the presented matrix is the identity:
2536	  \(\mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j, I)_{\bullet,\, I} = 1_{d\times d}\).
2537	\end{lemma}
2538	\begin{proof}
2539	  \uses{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one,
2540	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}
2541	  By injectivity (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_injective}) compare after
2542	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\). The minor presentation identity
2543	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor}) at \(J = I\) presents the
2544	  \(I\)-chart composite against its own inverse, which is the identity morphism, i.e.\
2545	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(1_{d\times d})\) (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one}).
2546	\end{proof}
2547	
2548	\begin{lemma}[The classifying map realises the universal matrix as the presented matrix]
2549	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix}
2550	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix}
2551	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_presentedMatrix,
2552	    lem:gr_presentedMatrix_submatrix_self}
2553	  Applying entrywise the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra map \(\mathrm{aeval}(M)\) -- which sends
2554	  the free variables of \(X^I\) to the complementary entries of
2555	  \(M = \mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, j, I)\) -- to the universal matrix \(X^I\)
2556	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) returns \(M\):
2557	  \(\mathrm{aeval}(M)(X^I) = M\).
2558	\end{lemma}
2559	\begin{proof}
2560	  \uses{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_submatrix_self}
2561	  Entrywise. On a complementary (free) entry \(x^I_{p,q}\) with \(q \notin I\),
2562	  \(\mathrm{aeval}(M)\) returns the corresponding entry of \(M\) by the variable-evaluation
2563	  rule. On an \(I\)-column entry the universal matrix has the identity minor, so the value
2564	  there is the corresponding entry of \(1_{d\times d}\), which matches the \(I\)-minor of
2565	  \(M\) -- the identity by \cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_submatrix_self}.
2566	\end{proof}

2568	\begin{lemma}[Transport of the image matrix along a ring homomorphism]
2569	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_ringHom}
2570	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_map_ringHom}
2571	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_universal_matrix, lem:gr_minorDet_unit,
2572	    lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
2573	  For size-\(d\) subsets \(I, J\) and any ring homomorphism
2574	  \(\mathrm{incl} : R^I_J \to D\) out of the localised overlap ring whose composite with
2575	  the structure map \(R^I \to R^I_J\) equals \(g\), pushing the image matrix
2576	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forward along \(\mathrm{incl}\) yields \(\bigl((g_{*}X^I)_J\bigr)^{-1}
2577	  (g_{*}X^I)\), where \(g_{*}X^I\) is \(X^I\) base-changed along \(g\):
2578	  \[
2579	    \mathrm{incl}_{*}\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr)
2580	      \;=\; \bigl((g_{*}X^I)_J\bigr)^{-1}\, (g_{*}X^I).
2581	  \]
2582	\end{lemma}
2583	\begin{proof}
2584	  \uses{lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
2585	  The image matrix is the product of the Cramer inverse \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\)
2586	  (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) with \(X^I\); ring homomorphisms commute with matrix
2587	  multiplication, with the submatrix (minor) operation, and -- because the minor
2588	  determinant \(P^I_J\) is a unit (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}) -- with the nonsingular
2589	  inverse (\cref{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}). Pushing each factor forward along
2590	  \(\mathrm{incl}\) and rewriting the structure-map composite as \(g\) gives the stated
2591	  base-changed expression.
2592	\end{proof}
2593	
2594	\begin{lemma}[Naturality of the chart morphism]
2595	  \label{lem:gr_comp_chartMorphism}
2596	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.comp_chartMorphism}
2597	  \uses{def:gr_chartMorphism, lem:gr_chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix,
2598	    lem:gr_presentedMatrix_comp}
2599	  For \(p : W \to T_I\) on which the pulled-back \(I\)-chart composite is invertible, the
2600	  composite \(p \circ \varphi_I : W \to U^I\) is the morphism classified by the matrix
2601	  presenting the quotient along \(p \circ \iota_{T_I}\) -- i.e.\ classified by
2602	  \(\mathrm{presentedMatrix}(x, p \circ \iota_{T_I}, I)\).
2603	\end{lemma}
2604	\begin{proof}
2605	  \uses{lem:gr_chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix, lem:gr_presentedMatrix_comp}
2606	  Naturality of the \(\Gamma\)--\(\Spec\) classifying map turns \(p \circ \varphi_I\) into
2607	  the morphism classified by the \(p\)-restriction of the chart matrix. The chart matrix is
2608	  the presented matrix along \(\iota_{T_I}\) (\cref{lem:gr_chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix}),
2609	  and its \(p\)-restriction is the presented matrix along \(p \circ \iota_{T_I}\)
2610	  (\cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_comp}); the two classifying maps therefore coincide.
2611	\end{proof}

2613	\begin{lemma}[Chart-point identity over a commutative ring]
2614	  \label{lem:gr_chart_point_eq}
2615	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chart_point_eq}
2616	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
2617	  Let \(K\) be a commutative ring and \(f : R^I \to K\) a ring map under which the minor
2618	  \(P^I_J\) is a unit. Then the transported classifying map
2619	  \(\mathrm{lift}(f) \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}\) presents the same \(K\)-point through chart
2620	  \(J\) as \(f\) does through chart \(I\):
2621	  \[
2622	    \Spec(\mathrm{lift}(f) \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}) \circ \iota_J
2623	      \;=\; \Spec(f) \circ \iota_I.
2624	  \]
2625	\end{lemma}
2626	\begin{proof}
2627	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
2628	  Factor \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) as the chart transition followed by the chart inclusion
2629	  (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}); the glue condition of the glue data
2630	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) makes the transition square commute, and the localisation
2631	  lift recovers \(f\) after the chart inclusion. Assembling the two reductions identifies
2632	  the two composites into the glued scheme.
2633	\end{proof}

2635	\begin{lemma}[Overlap compatibility of the chart morphisms]
2636	  \label{lem:gr_chartMorphism_glue_compat}
2637	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartMorphism_glue_compat}
2638	  \uses{def:gr_chartMorphism, def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp,
2639	    lem:gr_comp_chartMorphism, lem:gr_presentedMatrix_changeOfBasis,
2640	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix, lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_ringHom,
2641	    lem:gr_chart_point_eq, lem:gr_isUnit_of_isIso_matrixEndRect,
2642	    lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor}
2643	  On the intersection \(P = T_I \times_T T_J\) the two composites of the chart morphisms
2644	  with the glue-data immersions agree:
2645	  \[
2646	    \mathrm{pr}_1 \circ (\varphi_I \circ \iota_I)
2647	      \;=\; \mathrm{pr}_2 \circ (\varphi_J \circ \iota_J).
2648	  \]
2649	  This is the gluing hypothesis from which \(\mathrm{grPointOfRankQuotient}(x)\) is
2650	  assembled.
2651	\end{lemma}
2652	\begin{proof}
2653	  \uses{lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp, lem:gr_comp_chartMorphism,
2654	    lem:gr_presentedMatrix_changeOfBasis, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix,
2655	    lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_ringHom, lem:gr_chart_point_eq,
2656	    lem:gr_isUnit_of_isIso_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor}
2657	  On \(P\) both chart composites pull back to isomorphisms
2658	  (\cref{lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp}), so the pulled-back quotient is presented by both
2659	  matrices \(M^I_P\) and \(M^J_P\); through \cref{lem:gr_comp_chartMorphism} the two
2660	  composites are classified by these matrices. The \(J\)-minor of \(M^I_P\) is invertible
2661	  (\cref{lem:gr_isUnit_of_isIso_matrixEndRect},
2662	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor}), and the change-of-basis identity
2663	  \(M^I_P = (M^I_P)_J \cdot M^J_P\) (\cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_changeOfBasis}) --
2664	  realised on the universal matrices via
2665	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix} and the image-matrix transport
2666	  \cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_ringHom} -- identifies the \(J\)-classifying ring map with
2667	  the localisation-transport \(\mathrm{lift}(\mathrm{aeval}\,M^I_P) \circ
2668	  \tilde\theta_{I,J}\) of the \(I\)-classifying map. The glue-condition identity
2669	  (\cref{lem:gr_chart_point_eq}) then closes the square into the glued scheme.
2670	\end{proof}

2672	\begin{definition}[The morphism induced by a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2673	  \label{def:grPointOfRankQuotient}
2674	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.grPointOfRankQuotient}
2675	  \uses{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover, lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map,
2676	    def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle,
2677	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_chartComposite, def:gr_chartMatrix,
2678	    def:gr_chartMorphism, lem:gr_chartMorphism_glue_compat}
2679	  Let \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) be a rank-\(d\) quotient of
2680	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\) on a scheme \(T\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}). The morphism
2681	  \[
2682	    \mathrm{grPointOfRankQuotient}(x) \;:\; T \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
2683	  \]
2684	  is constructed as follows. Over each chart locus \(T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I)\)
2685	  (\cref{def:chartLocus}) the chart composite \(s_I \circ q\) is an isomorphism
2686	  (\cref{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}), so trivialising \(\mathcal{F}|_{T_I}\) by its
2687	  inverse presents \(q|_{T_I}\) as a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) of global functions on
2688	  \(T_I\) whose \(I\)-minor is the identity. Its \(d(r-d)\) complementary entries determine a
2689	  unique \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to \Gamma(T_I,
2690	  \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\), \(x^I_{p,q} \mapsto M^I_{p,q}\), hence by the \(\Gamma\)--\(\Spec\)
2691	  adjunction a unique \emph{chart morphism} \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
2692	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\), and thus
2693	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q|_{T_I}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}). The chart loci cover \(T\)
2694	  (\cref{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}), and on each overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two
2695	  presenting matrices are change-of-basis related by the invertible \(I\)-columns minor,
2696	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)

2774	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

2789	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
2790	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map

2793	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2794	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
2795	    \qquad
2796	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
2797	      \;\longmapsto\;
2798	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,

2800	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
2801	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
